questions.specification:
Fiji is an archipelago located in the South Pacific, known for its thriving tourism industry and longstanding sugar sector. Tourism directly and indirectly accounts for nearly 38% of Fiji’s gross domestic product (GDP), making it one of the country’s main sources of foreign exchange. The island nation receives over 800 000 international visitors in a normal (non-pandemic) year, with most tourists arriving from Australia and New Zealand. However, dependence on tourism also makes Fiji vulnerable to external shocks such as global economic downturns or natural disasters.

The sugar industry is the second-largest contributor to Fiji’s export earnings, employing workers in growing, harvesting, and processing sugarcane. Due to changing weather patterns and competition from other sugar-producing nations, sugar production in Fiji faces challenges in expanding supply. In an effort to diversify government revenue, Fiji applies a 9% value added tax (VAT) on domestic sugar sales.

In 2022, the Fijian government announced a 200 million FJD infrastructure investment program aimed at improving rural roads, upgrading port facilities, and modernizing sugar processing plants. Economists estimate Fiji’s marginal propensity to consume (MPC) at 0.75, suggesting a potentially significant boost to aggregate demand if the infrastructure spending is effectively implemented.

**Table 1: Key Macroeconomic Indicators for Fiji (2019–2020)**

| Indicator                      |   2019  |   2020  |
|--------------------------------|--------:|--------:|
| Real GDP (FJD millions)        |  11 500 |  11 845 |
| Population (thousands)         |     889 |     895 |
| Inflation rate (%)             |     1.5 |     1.0 |
| Gini coefficient               |    0.37 |    0.36 |

**Table 2: Sugar Market Data in Fiji**

| Year | Price (FJD/ton) | Quantity Demanded (million tons) | Quantity Supplied (million tons) |
|-----:|----------------:|---------------------------------:|---------------------------------:|
| 2021 |             800 |                             1.20 |                             1.05 |
| 2022 |             840 |                             1.10 |                             1.04 |

Additional Information  

• Fiji’s VAT on sugar is 9%.  
• The government’s total planned infrastructure investment in 2022 is 200 million FJD.  
• Economists estimate Fiji’s MPC = 0.75.  
• Corporate tax rate is 20%.  
• Personal income tax is a progressive system up to 20%.

parts.content: Using information from Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for sugar in Fiji when the price increases from 800 FJD per ton in 2021 to 840 FJD per ton in 2022.
parts.markscheme:
• Percentage change in quantity demanded = [(1.10 – 1.20) / 1.20] × 100 = –8.33%  
• Percentage change in price = [(840 – 800) / 800] × 100 = 5%  
• PED = (–8.33%) / (5%) = –1.67

parts.content: Using information from the text above, calculate the total change in real GDP resulting from the government’s 200 million FJD infrastructure investment, given the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) of 0.75.
parts.markscheme:
• Multiplier = 1 / (1 – MPC) = 1 / (1 – 0.75) = 4  
• Total change in GDP = 4 × 200 million FJD = 800 million FJD

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the real GDP growth rate for Fiji from 2019 to 2020.
parts.markscheme:
• Change in real GDP = 11 845 – 11 500 = 345 million FJD  
• Growth rate = (345 / 11 500) × 100 ≈ 3.0%

parts.content: Using information from Table 2 and the text above, calculate the total indirect tax (VAT) revenue from sugar sales in 2022.
parts.markscheme:
• Total expenditure on sugar = 840 FJD / ton × 1.10 million tons = 924 million FJD  
• VAT revenue = 924 million FJD × 9% = 83.16 million FJD

parts.content: Define the term “Keynesian multiplier.”
parts.markscheme: • The Keynesian multiplier is the factor by which an initial change in spending leads to a proportionally larger change in national income or real GDP.

parts.content: Explain why Fiji’s sugar producers might have a relatively price-inelastic supply in the short run.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/bef2b612-60e9-4752-b382-8843c66ae25d)

• Sugar production requires significant time to plant, grow, and harvest sugarcane, reducing immediate responsiveness to price changes.  
• Limited availability of suitable agricultural land and specialized machinery constrains the ability to increase output quickly.  
• Sugar processing plants involve large capital investments, so adjusting capacity in the short run is difficult.  
• The short-run production period makes it challenging to recruit and train additional workers quickly, contributing to inelastic supply.  

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explaination</marks>

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explaination</marks>

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the percentage change in Fiji's real GDP per capita between 2019 and 2020. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
- Real GDP per capita 2019: 11,500 ÷ 889 = 12.94 thousand FJD
- Real GDP per capita 2020: 11,845 ÷ 895 = 13.24 thousand FJD
- Percentage change: (13.24 – 12.94) ÷ 12.94 × 100 ≈ 2.3%

parts.content: Using data from Table 1, explain how a reduction in the Gini coefficient might benefit Fiji’s long-term economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
• The Gini coefficient in Fiji decreased from 0.37 to 0.36, indicating a decrease in income inequality.  
• Lower inequality can help increase access to education and health for lower-income groups, raising the overall productivity of the labor force.  
• Increased purchasing power among a broader base of consumers can stimulate domestic demand and investment.  
• Greater social cohesion and reduced social tensions can support a stable environment for long-term economic growth.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which could be implemented by the government of Fiji in order to reduce the country’s vulnerability to external shocks arising from tourism and sugar exports.
parts.markscheme:
• Diversification is the process of broadening the range of economic activities to reduce reliance on a narrow set of industries.  
• External shocks are unforeseeable events that can disrupt exports of goods or services, causing unstable growth and foreign exchange earnings.  
• A supply-side policy such as providing tax incentives for new manufacturing or agricultural industries would help create additional export sectors beyond tourism and sugar.  
• Implementing targeted subsidies for developing alternative crops or supporting high-value agribusiness could spread risk and stabilize national income when global tourism demand falls or sugar prices decline. 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/4a69fc4a-092a-4091-a603-4b1d789c4fd3)
• Greater investment in infrastructure, already highlighted by the 200 million FJD program, can be directed toward supporting logistics, transport, and technology that benefit multiple sectors.  
• A broadened production base can ensure that abrupt falls in tourist arrivals or sugar export revenue are offset by other forms of export earnings.  
• Reliance on robust domestic and regional markets may reduce susceptibility to global economic downturns or natural disasters affecting tourism and sugarcane yields.  
• Improved human capital through training programs and research grants can boost innovation in new industries, increasing the country’s resilience.  
• Drawing from the case study, such policies lower the risk of sudden falls in GDP and foreign exchange receipts, mitigate high unemployment during tourism slumps, and ensure stable government revenue.  
• Over time, a more diverse economy would maintain steady growth, support inclusive development, and reduce long-term vulnerability to global shocks.

parts.content: Explain how international trade can reduce prices for domestic consumers.
parts.markscheme:
**Answers may include:**

### Definitions
- **International Trade**: The exchange of goods and services across national borders.
- **Comparative Advantage**: The ability of a country to produce a good or service at a lower opportunity cost than another country.
- **Competition**: A market situation in which multiple firms strive to attract consumers, often leading to improvements in efficiency and lower prices.

### Explanation/Economic Theory
- Domestic producers operating without international trade have fewer incentives to reduce production costs, since competition is limited to local firms.
- With international trade, foreign firms can enter the domestic market, intensifying competition.
  - This increased competition puts downward pressure on prices, as firms aim to attract consumers by offering more competitive pricing.
- Furthermore, access to a wider variety of inputs and resources (either cheaper or higher quality) through trade can lead to lower average costs of production.
  - These lower costs of production enable producers to reduce final goods’ prices for consumers.
- Moreover, comparative advantage allows countries to specialize in the production of goods and services where they have lower opportunity costs.
  - This specialization maximizes global output and leads to a more efficient allocation of resources, consequently lowering prices for importing countries.
- As domestic producers face competition from imports, they may also increase efficiency, adopt new technology, and benefit from economies of scale.
  - This larger-scale production and innovation help reduce per-unit costs, further decreasing prices for consumers.
- Ultimately, imported goods often enter the domestic market at a lower world price ($P_w$) than the existing domestic equilibrium price ($P_e$).
  - Domestic consumers then gain from access to these lower-priced imports, increasing consumer surplus (the difference between what consumers are willing to pay and what they actually pay).

### Diagram
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/d53c6dec-e017-458c-8f71-980da723deb4)

### Conclusion
- International trade can thus reduce prices for domestic consumers through increased competition, efficient resource allocation (specialization and comparative advantage), economies of scale, and technology adoption, resulting in greater consumer surplus and welfare.

parts.content: Using real-world examples, evaluate the limits of the theory of comparative advantage.
parts.markscheme:
**Answers may include:**

### Definition
- **Comparative advantage**: When a country can produce a good or service at a lower opportunity cost than another country, leading to potential gains from trade.
- **Opportunity cost**: The next best alternative foregone when an economic decision is made.
- **Specialization**: The concentration of the productive efforts of individuals, firms, or countries on a limited number of activities or goods.

### Explanation/Economic Theory
- **Foundation of the Theory**
  - Comparative advantage suggests that if two countries specialize in producing goods where they have a lower opportunity cost, then overall production and consumption can increase.
  - It also assumes minimal trade barriers, perfect mobility of resources, and constant returns to scale.

- **Opportunity Cost and Specialization**
  - Comparative advantage is driven by the concept of opportunity cost: by specializing in goods that have the lowest opportunity cost, countries can maximize efficiency.
  - Through specialization, each country allocates resources to produce the good it can produce relatively more efficiently, trading for goods it cannot produce as efficiently.
  - This, in turn, raises global output and can lead to mutually beneficial trade if prices reflect comparative costs accurately.

- **Potential Benefits**
  - **Greater economic efficiency**: Resources are used where they are most productive.
  - **Increased variety and lower prices**: Specialization and trade can result in expanded consumer choice and competitive prices.
  - **Expanded markets**: Opening domestic producers to international markets can lead to higher export earnings and growth.

- **Assumptions of Comparative Advantage**
  - **No transport costs**: In reality, freight and other costs may reduce or eliminate gains from trade.
  - **Perfect knowledge**: Producers and consumers may not have full information, affecting trade decisions.
  - **Mobility of factors**: Labor and capital are assumed to shift easily between industries, which is not always feasible.
  - **No trade barriers**: Tariffs, quotas, and other protectionist measures can restrict specialization.

- **Limits/Weaknesses in the Real World**
  - **Structural unemployment**: Shifting resources from less efficient industries can leave workers temporarily jobless if retraining is slow.
  - **Income distribution issues**: Gains from trade may not be distributed evenly, increasing inequalities.
  - **Environmental concerns**: Specializing in resource-intensive production can harm ecological balance, and the theory does not account for negative externalities.
  - **Dynamic comparative advantage**: Over time, countries can develop new capabilities (through technology or education), making initial comparative advantages obsolete.

### Diagram
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7df57ddd-dd21-4698-998e-0e623dbcd2fe)

### Evaluation

- **Short-run and Long-run Impacts on Stakeholders**
  - In the **short run**, specialization can benefit firms in industries with a clear comparative advantage but can harm sectors facing foreign competition, leading to layoffs.
  - Conversely, in the **long run**, continued specialization can spur economic growth if supported by technological upgrades, but reliance on a narrow range of exports may expose producers and consumers to external shocks (e.g., sudden price drops in commodity markets).

- **Real World Examples**
  - **China and electronics manufacturing (post-2000)**: China’s rising dominance in electronics exports stems from relatively lower labor costs and a developing high-tech manufacturing base. This supports the idea of comparative advantage, but there have been critiques regarding wage inequalities and environmental degradation.
  - **Bangladesh’s garment industry (2010s)**: Bangladesh capitalized on low labor costs to become a major exporter of apparel. While this provides jobs and growth, safety and labor rights remain concerns, showing that the theory’s assumption of overall welfare gains may overlook working conditions.
  - **United States and high-technology services (2000s onwards)**: The US has specialized in software and advanced services due to a comparative advantage in skilled labor. However, outsourcing of manufacturing can lead to job losses in traditional industries, illustrating how structural unemployment can challenge the theoretical predictions.
  - **Latin American commodity exports**: Countries like Chile depend heavily on copper exports. When global copper prices fall, government revenue and employment can decline drastically, highlighting the risk of overdependence on a single comparative advantage.

### Conclusion
- The theory of comparative advantage offers a powerful explanation of how trade can foster greater global output and efficiency.
- Nevertheless, real-world complexities: such as transport costs, trade barriers, environmental concerns, and uneven gain, limit its direct applicability.
- Overall, while comparative advantage underpins many trade agreements, policymakers often need to address its potential downsides, including environmental and social impacts, to optimize trade outcomes.

questions.specification:
Greenland, an autonomous territory of Denmark, is the world’s largest island with a population of about 56000 people. The economy relies heavily on fisheries (accounting for more than 90% of Greenland’s total exports), public sector services financed through grants from Denmark, and (increasingly) tourism. Recent explorations suggest that Greenland has untapped reserves of minerals and rare earth elements. However, high infrastructure costs and environmental considerations pose challenges to diversification. 

Real GDP growth has been volatile due to changes in global demand for fish products and fluctuations in fish prices, while the population faces income inequality concerns. Recent debates in Greenland’s Parliament (Inatsisartut) focus on reforms to taxation and public spending, seeking to foster inclusive economic growth and reduce income disparities.

Table 1: Key Macroeconomic Indicators of Greenland (2018–2021)

| Indicator                                | 2018  | 2019  | 2020  | 2021  |
|-----------------------------------------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| Nominal GDP (bn DKK)                    | 15.8  | 16.5  | 16.3  | 17.0  |
| Real GDP growth (%)                     | 2.4   | 3.0   | -1.2  | 2.1   |
| Unemployment rate (%)                   | 6.2   | 5.5   | 7.1   | 6.4   |
| Gini coefficient                        | 0.32  | 0.34  | 0.35  | 0.35  |
| Government budget balance (% of GDP)    | -2.2  | -1.5  | -4.0  | -3.0  |

Table 2: Fish Exports Data (2019–2021)

| Year | Fish exports (tonnes) | Average price per tonne (DKK) | Estimated PED for Greenlandic halibut |
|------|-----------------------|--------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| 2019 | 25 000               | 25 000                         | -0.8                                    |
| 2020 | 24 000               | 27 500                         | -0.7                                    |
| 2021 | 27 000               | 28 000                         | -0.6                                    |

Table 3: Income Distribution and Taxation(2021)

| Income group  | Share of total population (%) | Average annual income (DKK) | Tax rate (%) |
|--------------|-------------------------------|-----------------------------|--------------|
| Low-income    | 25                            | 140 000                     | 35           |
| Middle-income | 50                            | 250 000                     | 40           |
| High-income   | 25                            | 600 000                     | 45           |

Figure 1: Simplified market for Greenlandic Halibut (2021)

Prices are measured in DKK per tonne. Demand (D) and supply (S) represent domestic demand and supply. Pw1 is the initial world price of 28 000 DKK per tonne, while Pw2 is a possible world price of 30 000 DKK per tonne.

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate Greenland’s approximate nominal GDP per capita for 2021, given that the population was 56 000. Show your workings.
parts.markscheme:
- 17.0 billion DKK ÷ 56 000 = 17 000 000 000 ÷ 56 000 
- ≈ 303 571 DKK per capita 
- Final answer: approximately 304 000 DKK

parts.content: Based on Table 1, calculate Greenland’s average annual real GDP growth rate over the period 2018–2021 (use simple arithmetic mean of the four rates, treating the negative number for 2020 as part of the calculation). Show your workings.
Using the data from Table 2 for 2020 and 2021, calculate the percentage change in total export revenue (in DKK) from Greenlandic halibut.

Refer to Figure 1. Assume the price for Greenlandic halibut rises from Pw1 = 28 000 DKK per tonne to Pw2 = 30 000 DKK per tonne. Using the PED value of -0.6 for 2021, calculate the approximate percentage change in quantity demanded for Greenlandic halibut.
Define the term “progressive tax.”
parts.markscheme: - A progressive tax is a tax that takes a higher proportion of income from higher-income earners than from lower-income earners.

parts.content: Using a Keynesian multiplier diagram (AD/AS with an upward sloping AS), explain how an increase in government spending (funded partly by Danish block grants) could affect Greenland’s real GDP in the short run.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/dd35334e-a28f-472c-aaf7-5cc2bb6c4450)

- An increase in government spending raises aggregate demand, shifting the AD curve to the right, from AD1 to AD2.  
- With an upward sloping AS, real GDP increases and there may be some upward pressure on the price level (pl1 -> pl2).  
- The multiplier effect means that the initial rise in government spending leads to a larger final increase in national income.  
- In Greenland’s context, this can stimulate overall economic activity in the short run (Increased real GDP from Y to Yp), helping to address unemployment.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using Table 3, calculate the total income tax paid by the low-income group in Greenland in 2021. Assume the group consists of 25% of the 56 000 population and that everyone earns the average income stated. Show your workings.
parts.markscheme:
Population in low-income group = 25% of 56 000 = 14 000

Average income per person = 140 000 DKK

Tax rate = 35%

Total tax paid = 14 000 × 140 000 × 0.35
= 686 000 000 DKK

parts.content: Using information from the text and Tables 1 and 3, explain two reasons why persistent inequality (as indicated by the Gini coefficient and tax data) could be harmful to Greenland’s long-term economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
- Inequality can reduce aggregate demand if lower-income groups have limited ability to spend, potentially slowing economic expansion, especially when 25% of the population receives only 140 000 DKK on average.  
- Inequality may restrict access to education or skills-training for lower-income groups, reducing the quality of the workforce and limiting future growth potential in Greenland’s emerging sectors

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which could be implemented by Greenland’s Parliament in order to reduce income inequality and support long-term economic growth. Justify your recommendation.
parts.markscheme:
- Define two or three key terms from the question or case study.  
  - “Income inequality” is the unequal distribution of income across different individuals or households in an economy.  
  - “Long-term economic growth” is an increase in real output over an extended period, reflecting improvements in an economy’s productive capacity.  

- Explain how the economic theory connects to the policy choice and embed evidence from the case.  
  - Progressive government spending on human capital can enhance equality of opportunity and raise productivity.  
  - Higher allocation of public funds (supported by Danish block grants) toward education and vocational training can improve labor skills, especially crucial in developing new sectors like tourism and mining.  
  - The diagram shows an outward shift of the production possibility curve (from PPC₁ to PPC₂), illustrating long-term economic growth driven by improved human capital. Increased investment in education and training raises worker productivity, allowing Greenland to produce more goods and services. This shift reflects gains in efficiency and capacity, particularly in emerging sectors like tourism and mining, while also enabling more equitable income distribution as more people access higher-skilled, better-paying jobs.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7eac7bc0-7f07-417e-88ad-f9e3644587bd)

  - Lower-income groups who face challenges in affording proper education or training are more likely to benefit from subsidized or free opportunities, thereby improving long-term productivity.  

- Evaluate potential effectiveness and refer explicitly to case information.  
  - The focus on expanding human capital may address workforce deficiencies in Greenland’s main sectors (fisheries, tourism) and foster diversification into minerals and rare earth extraction.  
  - Reducing inequality through increased skills training can lead to higher incomes for low-income groups, potentially lowering the Gini coefficient (which remains at 0.35).  
  - The policy faces opportunity costs because increased education spending can strain government finances (budget deficit stood at -3.0% of GDP in 2021).  
  - However, improving equality through human capital development is likely to have positive externalities, adding to sustainable growth and reducing reliance on a small number of exports.  

- Provide a justified recommendation.  
  - Implementing expanded education and vocational programs funded by progressive taxation or grants fosters equality and bolsters skill-intensive sectors.  
  - This policy is likely to raise productivity, reduce inequality, and generate stronger long-term economic performance, despite short-term fiscal pressures.

questions.specification:
**Trade Dynamics between Aridonia and Floristan**

Recent data from the Global Trade Council (GTC) highlights the shifting economic relationship between two neighboring nations, Aridonia and Floristan. Aridonia, a technologically advanced economy, has specialized heavily in the production of high-end Electronic Chips. Its manufacturing sector benefits from significant research and development (R&D) and a highly skilled workforce. Conversely, Floristan is characterized by its vast agricultural resources and a large labor force, making it highly efficient in the production of Textiles.

The GTC report suggests that through international trade, both nations have moved toward a more efficient allocation of global resources. Aridonia’s firms have been able to access larger international markets, allowing them to lower their average costs of production through mass manufacturing. Meanwhile, Floristan’s consumers have gained access to affordable high-tech goods that were previously too expensive to produce domestically.

However, the transition has not been without challenges. In Floristan, some domestic chip manufacturers have struggled to stay afloat as cheap imports from Aridonia flood the market. Critics argue that over-reliance on Textile exports may limit Floristan's long-term economic development by trapping it in a low-value-added sector. Proponents of free trade, however, point to the rapid transfer of technology and capital goods from Aridonia to Floristan, which they claim is essential for modernizing Floristan’s infrastructure and boosting its long-run growth potential.

Table 1 shows the maximum possible output of Electronic Chips and Textiles for each country if all resources are devoted to a single good. Table 2 illustrates the consumption levels of both nations after they have specialized according to their comparative advantage and engaged in trade.

**Table 1: Maximum Production (Units per worker/hour)**

| Country | Electronic Chips | Textiles |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Aridonia | 20 | 10 |
| Floristan | 5 | 15 |

**Table 2: Consumption after Specialization and Trade (Total Units)**

| Country | Electronic Chips | Textiles |

| Aridonia | 14 | 6 |
| Floristan | 6 | 9 |

parts.content: Define the term *comparative advantage*.
parts.markscheme:
The ability of a country to produce a good or service at a lower opportunity cost <marks>1 mark</marks> 

than another country. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: List two benefits of international trade for consumers in a country like Floristan.
parts.markscheme:
- Access to a wider variety of goods and services (increased choice). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Lower prices for goods that are produced more efficiently abroad. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Higher quality of goods due to increased international competition. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>Award Maximum 2 Marks</marks>

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the opportunity cost for Floristan to produce one unit of Electronic Chips.
parts.markscheme:
Opportunity cost is the amount of one good sacrificed to produce an additional unit of another.
- In Floristan, the resources used to produce 5 Chips could instead produce 15 Textiles.
- Opportunity cost of 1 Chip = $15 / 5$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Opportunity cost = 3 units of Textiles. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using a production possibility frontier (PPF) diagram for Aridonia, draw the impact of specialization and trade on Aridonia’s consumption possibilities.
parts.markscheme:
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-18e32abc-ccdc-4bb8-90df-471fd73fd0d9-1771937587718/5e95ee6b-7f20-456b-84a0-e37cc52400e5.jpeg)

- Axes correctly labeled (Electronic Chips and Textiles) and a linear PPF drawn from 20 (Chips) to 10 (Textiles). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- A 'Trade Line' or 'Consumption Possibility Curve' shown starting at 20 (Chips) but extending beyond the original PPF (e.g., to a point showing more textiles can be consumed). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explanation that trade allows the country to consume at a point outside its domestic PPF. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using a domestic demand and supply diagram for Electronic Chips in Floristan, explain the impact of opening to trade on consumer surplus.
parts.markscheme:
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-18e32abc-ccdc-4bb8-90df-471fd73fd0d9-1771937587718/a6d93717-e250-4711-bab1-383bb7aa1684.jpeg)

- Initially, Floristan is in equilibrium at $P_d$ (domestic price) and $Q_d$ (domestic quantity).
- When trade opens, Floristan can import Chips at the lower world price $P_w$.
- At $P_w$, domestic quantity demanded increases to $Q_2$, while domestic quantity supplied falls to $Q_1$.
- The fall in price from $P_d$ to $P_w$ leads to an increase in consumer surplus, represented by the area between $P_d$, $P_w$, and the demand curve.

<marks>Award 2 Marks for Diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>Award 4 Marks for Diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using Table 2 and information from the text, explain how both Aridonia and Floristan achieve gains from trade through specialization.
parts.markscheme:
- Based on Table 1, Aridonia has a comparative advantage in Chips (OC = 0.5 Textiles) while Floristan has it in Textiles (OC = 0.33 Chips). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- If both countries split resources 50/50 without trade, Aridonia would consume 10 Chips and 5 Textiles, while Floristan would consume 2.5 Chips and 7.5 Textiles.
- Looking at Table 2, after trade, Aridonia consumes 14 Chips and 6 Textiles, and Floristan consumes 6 Chips and 9 Textiles. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Both countries are consuming more of both goods than they could produce domestically in a balanced self-sufficiency model. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This represents a gain in welfare and material living standards due to the more efficient allocation of labor according to comparative advantage. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using a Long-Run Average Cost (LRAC) diagram, explain how international trade can lead to lower prices for consumers.
parts.markscheme:
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-18e32abc-ccdc-4bb8-90df-471fd73fd0d9-1771937587718/94ca4909-f983-4337-b36d-0d64e0fade97.jpeg)

- Initially, a firm produces for a small domestic market at point $A$ with high average costs $AC_A$.
- International trade opens up larger global markets, allowing the firm to increase its scale of production to point $B$.
- As output increases from $Q_A$ to $Q_B$, the firm benefits from economies of scale (e.g., bulk buying, technical efficiencies), causing average costs to fall to $AC_B$.
- These lower production costs can be passed on to consumers in the form of lower prices.

<marks>Award 2 Marks for Diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>Award 4 Marks for Diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using an AD-AS diagram, explain the possible impact of technology transfer from Aridonia on Floristan’s economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-18e32abc-ccdc-4bb8-90df-471fd73fd0d9-1771937587718/619c04cd-a272-4481-b7b4-83e2b3621bf6.jpeg)

- Technology transfer (e.g., importing advanced machinery or software) increases the quality and productivity of the factors of production in Floristan. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This increases the productive capacity of the economy, represented by a rightward shift of the Long-Run Aggregate Supply (LRAS) curve from $LRAS_1$ to $LRAS_2$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This shift leads to an increase in potential output (Real GDP) from $Y_1$ to $Y_2$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- An increase in real output over time constitutes economic growth. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>Award 2 Marks for Diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>Award 4 Marks for Diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the effects of specialization based on comparative advantage on the economic development of a country like Floristan.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### **Definition**  
- **Specialization**: When a nation focuses its productive efforts on a limited range of goods where it has a comparative advantage.  
- **Economic development**: A multidimensional process involving improvements in living standards, reduction in poverty, and structural changes in the economy.  

### **Arguments in Favor (Gains from Trade)**  
- **Increased Real Incomes**: As shown in Table 2, Floristan consumes more after trade (6 Chips, 9 Textiles) than it could produce alone. This increases material living standards.  
- **Resource Allocation**: By focusing on Textiles, Floristan uses its large labor force and land efficiently, generating export revenue to buy capital goods.  
- **Technology Transfer**: The text mentions the transfer of technology and capital goods from Aridonia. This can modernize Floristan’s industries and lead to higher productivity in other sectors (LRAS shift).  
- **Lower Prices**: Domestic consumers benefit from cheaper imports (e.g., Electronic Chips), increasing their disposable income for other developmental needs like education or health.

### **Arguments Against (Risks and Limitations)**  
- **Over-specialization**: Relying heavily on one sector (Textiles) makes Floristan vulnerable to changes in global demand or price fluctuations. If global textile prices fall, its export earnings will crash.  
- **Prebisch-Singer Hypothesis**: Primary products and low-value-added manufacturing (like textiles) often face declining terms of trade relative to high-tech goods (like chips) over time, potentially widening the gap between Aridonia and Floristan.  
- **Infant Industry Argument**: The text notes that domestic chip manufacturers in Floristan are struggling. Without protection, Floristan may never develop a high-tech sector, limiting its structural transformation.  
- **De-industrialization**: Job losses in the domestic manufacturing sectors that cannot compete with Aridonia could lead to regional unemployment and inequality.

### **Evaluation**  
- The benefits depend on the **nature of the good**. Textiles are low-value-added; if Floristan doesn't use the gains from trade to diversify, its development might stall.  
- **Government Policy**: The gains from trade only lead to development if the government invests export revenues into human capital (education) and infrastructure.  
- **Terms of Trade**: If the terms of trade favor Aridonia significantly, most of the gains from trade may be captured by the developed nation, leaving Floristan with minimal improvements in development.  
- **Conclusion**: While specialization provides immediate consumption gains (static efficiency), its long-term impact on development (dynamic efficiency) is uncertain and depends on whether it facilitates or hinders industrial diversification.

questions.specification:
**Persistent current account deficits challenge the Republic of Zamara’s stability**

The central bank of Zamara has expressed alarm over the nation's deteriorating current account deficit, which widened to USD 14.80 billion in 2022/23 from USD 6.20 billion the previous fiscal year. Economic analysts attribute this to a combination of stagnant export revenues and a surge in the volume of imported goods. Because of the significantly lower prices of manufactured electronics and luxury vehicles in global markets compared to domestic alternatives, the government has proposed implementing strict import quotas on these non-essential items to preserve foreign exchange reserves.

Zamara's Finance Minister supported the move, stating that the reliance on external borrowing to fund consumption is unsustainable. However, he cautioned that roughly 40% of the country's imports are capital goods and industrial machinery, which are vital for the expansion of the agricultural and green energy sectors, particularly for Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs).

To correct the trade imbalance, the Ministry of Planning has advocated for a managed depreciation of the Zar, the local currency. The Zar is currently estimated to be overvalued by approximately 25%. While depreciation could boost competitiveness, the Governor of the Central Bank warned that it might trigger cost-push inflation, particularly for fuel and food.

Despite these challenges, Zamara’s real GDP growth accelerated to 5.8% in 2023. To maintain this momentum, the government plans to double the share of total business loans allocated to SMEs from roughly 10% to 20% by 2025. Meanwhile, fiscal expansion has pushed public debt to 82% of GDP in 2023, well above the sustainability threshold of 70% recommended by international lenders.

Table 1: Zamara’s Current Account Data

| Year    | Exports (US$ billion) | Imports (US$ billion) | Current Account Deficit (US$ billion) |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------------|
| 2021/22 | 30.50                | 36.70                | 6.20                                |
| 2022/23 | 28.20                | 43.00                | 14.80                               |


Table 2: Key Economic Indicators

| Year | GDP Growth Rate (%) | SME Loans (% of total business loans) | Public Debt (% of GDP) | Zar Overvaluation (%) |
|------|----------------------|--------------------------------------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| 2022 | 4.2                  | 9.5                                  | 75                     | 15                      |
| 2023 | 5.8                  | 11.0                                 | 82                     | 25                      |

parts.content: Define the term "current account".
parts.markscheme:
The sum of <marks>1 mark</marks>

the balance of trade in goods and services, primary income (income), and secondary income (current transfers). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: List two fiscal policy measures.
parts.markscheme:
- Changes in direct or indirect taxation levels. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Changes in government spending on goods and services or infrastructure. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Changes in transfer payments (e.g., subsidies, welfare). <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>Award max 2 marks</marks>

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the percentage increase in Zamara’s current account deficit from 2021/22 to 2022/23.
parts.markscheme:
1. Percentage change formula and correct substitution of values:

$$\text{Percentage Increase} = \left(\frac{\text{New Deficit} - \text{Old Deficit}}{\text{Old Deficit}}\right) \times 100$$

$$= \left(\frac{14.80 - 6.20}{6.20}\right) \times 100$$

<marks>1 mark</marks>

2. Correct final answer:

$$= \left(\frac{8.60}{6.20}\right) \times 100$$

$$= 138.71\%$$

<marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Draw a diagram to show the effect of a low price of foreign goods on the quantity imported by Zamara.
parts.markscheme:
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-1fce7008-8ed2-4057-b09b-62bfafbe05c2-1771840759346/9efe570b-5bfc-46f5-8a7b-c5beddaf97f3.jpeg)

- Correct supply and demand diagram for a domestic market. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- World price ($$P_w$$) shown below the domestic equilibrium price ($$P_d$$), with the gap between domestic quantity supplied ($$Q_{s1}$$) and domestic quantity demanded ($$Q_{d1}$$) correctly identified as imports. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Axes (Price and Quantity) and curves (Domestic Supply and Demand) correctly labeled. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using an exchange rate diagram, explain how a depreciation of the Zar could impact Zamara’s trade balance.
parts.markscheme:
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-1fce7008-8ed2-4057-b09b-62bfafbe05c2-1771840759346/e994972e-3976-4496-8ea3-f58e3df7175e.jpeg)

- An exchange rate is the price of one currency in terms of another. 
- A depreciation is shown by an increase in the supply of Zar ($$S_1$$ to $$S_2$$) or a decrease in demand for Zar, leading to a lower exchange rate value ($$e_1$$ to $$e_2$$).
- As the Zar loses value, Zamara’s exports become cheaper for foreign consumers in their own currency, likely increasing export volumes.
- Conversely, imports become more expensive in terms of Zar, likely decreasing import volumes.
- Assuming the Marshall-Lerner condition holds, the trade balance (part of the current account) should improve as export revenues rise and import expenditures fall.

<marks>2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how an increase in government debt could affect economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-1fce7008-8ed2-4057-b09b-62bfafbe05c2-1771840759346/03f543ba-b1cd-40ce-8773-42c3221073f8.jpeg)

- High public debt (82% of GDP) often forces a government to reduce future spending to service debt interest payments or to comply with lender restrictions.
- A reduction in government spending ($$G$$), a component of aggregate demand ($$AD$$), shifts the AD curve to the left ($$AD_1$$ to $$AD_2$$).
- This results in a new equilibrium with lower real GDP ($$Y_f$$ to $$Y_2$$), representing a slowdown or contraction in economic growth.
- Additionally, high debt may lead to higher interest rates (crowding out), further reducing investment ($$I$$) and growth.

<marks>2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a demand and supply diagram, explain how restricting luxury imports may affect domestic production in Zamara.
parts.markscheme:
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-1fce7008-8ed2-4057-b09b-62bfafbe05c2-1771840759346/b0b1a86f-12a2-4596-81a6-30d6774d4a87.jpeg)

- An import quota is a physical limit on the quantity of a good that can be imported.
- The diagram shows that at the world price ($$P_w$$), domestic producers only supply $$Q_1$$. 
- By imposing a quota, the supply curve effectively shifts to the right by the amount of the quota ($$S_d + \text{Quota}$$).
- This results in a higher domestic price ($$P_q$$) than the world price.
- At this higher price, domestic producers are incentivized to increase their output from $$Q_1$$ to $$Q_2$$, thereby increasing domestic production and employment in that sector.

<marks>2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, evaluate Zamara's current measures effectiveness in achieving long-run economic growth and/or development.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### **Definitions**
- **Economic growth**: An increase in the real output of an economy over time (increase in real GDP).
- **Economic development**: A multidimensional process involving improvements in living standards, reduction in poverty, and increased access to essential services.

### **Analysis of Measures**

#### **Import Quotas on Luxury Goods**
- **Pros**: Reduces the current account deficit (which surged from $6.2B to $14.8B) by limiting foreign exchange outflows. Protects infant industries, potentially encouraging domestic manufacturing of electronics and vehicles, which creates jobs.
- **Cons**: May lead to higher domestic prices and reduced consumer surplus. Since 40% of imports are capital goods, any accidental restriction on industrial machinery could reduce the economy's productive capacity ($$LRAS$$), hindering long-term growth.

#### **Currency Depreciation (Managed)**
- **Pros**: Corrects the 25% overvaluation of the Zar. Makes Zamara’s exports (agricultural/green energy) more competitive globally, helping to narrow the trade gap and boost AD.
- **Cons**: Significant risk of cost-push inflation. As a developing nation, Zamara likely imports essential fuel and technology; a weaker currency makes these more expensive, potentially lowering real incomes and slowing development.

#### **Credit Expansion for SMEs (Targeting 20%)**
- **Pros**: SMEs are often the backbone of employment. Focusing on Green Energy SMEs promotes sustainable development and reduces long-term reliance on imported fossil fuels. Increased credit facilitates capital investment ($$I$$), shifting the $$LRAS$$ curve rightward for long-run growth.
- **Cons**: High risk of non-performing loans if SMEs lack management capacity. Success depends on the efficiency of the financial sector and the quality of the projects funded.

#### **Fiscal Challenges (Public Debt at 82%)**
- **Impact**: The high debt level limits the government’s ability to invest in "development" priorities like education and healthcare. If the government must implement austerity to repay debt, AD will fall, potentially leading to a recession (as shown in the AD/AS diagram).

### **Evaluation**
- **Short-run vs. Long-run**: Quotas and depreciation offer a "quick fix" for the current account but do not address the underlying lack of export diversity. Long-run success depends on whether the SME credit expansion actually improves productivity.
- **Trade-offs**: There is a conflict between managing the external deficit (via depreciation/quotas) and maintaining price stability (inflation control).
- **Conclusion**: While the focus on SMEs and correcting currency overvaluation are steps toward sustainable growth, the looming debt crisis and potential for inflation from depreciation pose significant risks. For development to occur, growth must be inclusive and the government must find a way to stabilize the debt-to-GDP ratio without crippling essential public services.

questions.specification:
**Northland Free Trade Agreement**

Northland is a landlocked developing country of 5 million people. Despite agriculture employing 60% of the labor force, the sector contributes only 25% to GDP, highlighting significant productivity challenges. The country's trade profile reflects this economic structure, with exports concentrated in primary products like coffee, tea, and textiles, while relying heavily on imports of machinery, oil, and processed foods, resulting in a persistent annual trade deficit of $1 billion.

In response to growing concerns about import dependency and the need to protect domestic industries, Northland's government initially implemented substantial tariffs on processed food imports. This protection policy aimed to nurture local food processing capabilities and reduce the trade deficit. However, while these measures provided some shelter for domestic producers, they also resulted in higher consumer prices and limited market competition.

Recently, Northland has taken a significant step by signing a free trade agreement (FTA) with a neighboring country, marking a shift from protectionist policies toward regional integration. This move presents both opportunities and challenges: while it promises improved market access for Northland's exports and potential technology transfer through increased regional trade, it also exposes local producers to stronger competition. However, to maintain domestic standards of living in face of fear of machinery replacing jobs, the Northland's government has imposed a minimum wage. The success of these policy shifts will largely depend on how well Northland's agricultural sector can adapt to new market pressures while leveraging its traditional export strengths.

Table 1: Northland's Trade Data Before and After FTA

| Year | Exports (US$ billion) | Imports (US$ billion) | Trade Deficit (US$ billion) |
|------|----------------------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| 2021 | 2.5                  | 3.5                  | 1.0                        |
| 2023 | 3.2                  | 3.8                  | 0.6                        |


Table 2: Price Elasticities of Demand for Northland’s Key Exports and Imports

| Product                 | PED  | PES  |
|-------------------------|------|------|
| Coffee                 | -0.6 | 0.4  |
| Tea                    | -0.8 | 0.6  |
| Textiles               | -1.2 | 1.0  |
| Machinery (imported)   | -0.3 | 0.2  |
| Processed food (imported) | -0.9 | 0.7  |

- Upwards shift in world supply curve, change in imported quantity shown (student may also add the exact quantities from the text). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Axes and curves correctly labeled, government revenue, and welfare loss. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using a tariff diagram, explain how the removal of tariffs on processed food might affect consumption in Northland.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a7f53533-6a24-4f56-b53e-6d7c8b713196)

- With the removal of tariffs on processed food, the world price for processed food in Northland will shift downwards, from $P_w + t$ to $P_w$, due to an increase in the quantity supplied at each price level. 
- This results in an increase in quantity demanded (from $Q_1$ to $Q_2$) as cheaper prices lead to more consumers being able and willing to buy processed food.


<marks>2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a comparative advantage diagram, explain how the FTA could improve Northland’s export competitiveness.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/791db94c-f30b-433c-b348-0c447e9a76e5)

With reference to the comparative advantage diagram:

- The production possibility curve (PPC) for Northland is shown with two goods (e.g., coffee and machinery). Initially, Northland may not be specializing in the good that it has a comparative advantage in.
- The FTA allows Northland to specialize in producing and exporting the good in which it has a lower opportunity cost (e.g., coffee). This specialization leads to a more efficient allocation of resources, increasing the quantity of coffee produced and improving export competitiveness.
- Further as Northland’s specialization in coffee improves, it can trade more efficiently with the neighboring country, increasing its exports and gaining access to a larger regional market, leading to enhanced export competitiveness.

<marks>2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a free trade diagram, explain how increased imports could impact Northland’s domestic food processing industry.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/78f5ebf6-23da-4550-963c-3a1c7479f50c)

- In the scenario where free trade is achieved due to removal of protectionism policies, the domestic price for processed food decreases from $P_d$ to $P_w$ as foreign firms are more efficient and hence produce processed foods at much cheaper prices. 
- This leads to domestic producers being limited to produce upto $Q_d$ as producing any further quantities lead to costs of production which are higher than the price (leading to loss). Therefore, domestic producer revenue reduces from the equilibrium price to $Q_d \times P_w$. 
- Further, $Q_d$ to $Q_s$ of demand is fulfilled by foreign producers. 
- Therefore, increased imports lead to an immense loss in supply, and hence revenue for domestic processed food producers as they are not competitive compared to foreign producers.

<marks>2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a labour market diagram, explain how the imposition of a minimum wage might lead to structural unemployment in Northland’s agricultural sector.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7b280307-249e-4967-8306-e4375106997f)

- A minimum wage is a type of price floor set above the equilibrium wage (We).
- In the labour market diagram, the imposition of a minimum wage (above the equilibrium wage) at $W_{min}$ creates a situation where the quantity of labour supplied ($Q_s$) exceeds the quantity of labour demanded ($Q_d$).
- This results in structural unemployment, as employers in Northland's agricultural sector may not be able to afford to hire as many workers at the higher wage, leading to a mismatch between available jobs and workers willing to work at the minimum wage.

<marks>2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the potential impact of price elasticities on Northland’s trade balance.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### **Definition**  
- **Price Elasticity of Demand (PED)**: Measures the responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in price. A PED > 1 indicates elastic demand, while PED < 1 indicates inelastic demand.  
- **Price Elasticity of Supply (PES)**: Measures the responsiveness of quantity supplied to a change in price. A low PES indicates producers cannot respond quickly to price changes.  
- **Trade balance**: The difference between the value of a country's exports and imports. A trade deficit occurs when imports exceed exports.

### **Effect of Export Elasticities on Export Revenue**  
#### **Inelastic Demand for Agricultural Exports**  
- Northland’s key export goods — **coffee (PED = -0.6)** and **tea (PED = -0.8)** — are **price inelastic**, meaning a **fall in price leads to a proportionally smaller increase in quantity demanded**, causing **export revenue to fall**.  
- In the context of trade liberalisation via the **FTA**, increased supply from both countries could **lower global prices**, worsening **export earnings** if demand does not rise proportionally.  
- **PES for coffee (0.4)** and **tea (0.6)** are also inelastic, limiting Northland’s ability to expand output in response to rising demand, **constraining potential gains** from improved market access.

#### **Diagram**: Inelastic Demand and Revenue  
- When demand is inelastic, a price drop causes **total revenue to fall** (TR = P × Q), illustrated by a **steep demand curve**.  
- Even if the FTA increases demand, **limited supply responsiveness** means **little export revenue growth**, especially in the **short run**.

#### **Limitations**  
- Over time, **technology transfer** and improved infrastructure from the FTA may increase **PES**, enabling better export performance.  
- **Textiles (PED = -1.2, PES = 1.0)** have elastic demand and supply, so price reductions **increase export revenue**, making textiles the **most promising export** post-FTA.

### **Impact of Import Elasticities on Import Spending**  
#### **Inelastic Demand for Imported Machinery**  
- **Machinery imports have very inelastic demand (PED = -0.3)**, meaning **price increases cause only a small drop in quantity demanded**, so **import spending remains high**.  
- **PES = 0.2** indicates that even foreign producers will not increase supply significantly if prices rise, keeping prices high and **import dependency persistent**.  
- Northland’s **FTA may reduce tariffs**, causing machinery prices to fall and quantity demanded to rise. But because of low PED, the **value of imports may increase**, worsening the trade balance in the short run.

#### **Processed Food Imports and Price Sensitivity**  
- **Processed food imports have PED = -0.9**, closer to unitary elasticity. If prices fall post-FTA (e.g., due to tariff removal), **quantity demanded increases proportionally**, leading to **minimal net change in import spending**.  
- But this also **intensifies competition** for domestic producers, who previously relied on **tariff protection**. This could **reduce domestic output**, possibly increasing **long-term dependence on imports**.

#### **Diagram**: Tariff Removal and Import Expenditure  
- A reduction in tariffs lowers the price of imported goods. When **PED < 1**, **import expenditure rises**. The total area under the **import demand curve** expands more on the price side than the quantity side.

### **Trade Balance Outcomes and the Marshall-Lerner Condition**  
#### **Elasticity and Trade Balance Improvement**  
- **Marshall-Lerner condition** states that a **depreciation or trade policy improving export competitiveness** will improve the trade balance if the **sum of PEDs of exports and imports > 1**.  
- For **coffee and machinery**, PED_sum = 0.6 + 0.3 = 0.9 → **trade balance unlikely to improve** for these items.  
- For **textiles and processed food**, PED_sum = 1.2 + 0.9 = 2.1 → **trade balance likely to improve** with increased exports and controlled imports.

#### **Evidence from Data**  
- **Trade deficit improved from US$1.0B in 2021 to US$0.6B in 2023** (Table 1), suggesting **elastic exports like textiles** may be contributing positively.  
- Increased **regional market access** due to the FTA could be boosting **textile exports**, which have **high PED and PES**, allowing **greater quantity response** to price shifts.

### **Long-Term Structural Considerations**  
#### **Agricultural Productivity Constraints**  
- Although agriculture employs **60% of Northland’s labor force**, it contributes only **25% of GDP**, indicating **low productivity**.  
- With inelastic supply and demand for key exports, **short-term gains from the FTA are limited** unless supported by **investment in irrigation, storage, and logistics**.

#### **Minimum Wage Policy Effects**  
- Imposing a **minimum wage** could raise **production costs**, reducing **competitiveness** of domestic industries if **not offset by productivity gains**, especially in **labor-intensive sectors** like **processed foods**.  
- This may worsen the **import-competing position**, particularly if **elastic imports** increase.

### **Overall Evaluation**  
#### **Advantages**  
- **Textiles**, with **elastic PED and PES**, offer the greatest **export revenue growth potential**, supporting **trade balance improvement**.  
- Trade liberalisation under the FTA can enhance **market access**, encourage **technology transfer**, and **stimulate export diversification**.  
- The improvement in the **trade deficit from US$1B to US$0.6B** indicates a **partial gain**, likely from **elastic export sectors**.

#### **Disadvantages**  
- **Key exports (coffee and tea)** have **inelastic demand and supply**, limiting the **positive impact of price changes** on export revenues.  
- **Import spending on machinery** may rise, given **low PED**, worsening trade balance unless domestic production capacity increases.  
- Gains may be **undermined by structural inefficiencies**, unless supply-side reforms accompany trade liberalisation.

#### **Conclusion**  
The impact of price elasticities on Northland’s trade balance is **mixed**. While **textiles** show promising improvement due to high elasticity, **agriculture and essential imports** remain constrained by inelastic responses. To sustain improvements in the trade balance, Northland must combine trade openness with **supply-side investment**, **export diversification**, and **productivity gains**. Elasticities matter, but only alongside **structural transformation**.

questions.specification:
## Nigeria-Morocco Trade Negotiations

Text A — Background and Economic Overview  

Nigeria, located in West Africa, is one of the continent’s largest economies with an estimated GDP of US$450 billion in 2022. It has a population of over 210 million people, and between 2018 and 2022, the average inflation rate rose from 9.3% to 16.9%. The government has responded by tightening monetary policy and increasing spending on infrastructure to stimulate employment and reduce poverty. However, concerns remain about the country’s over-reliance on crude oil exports, which account for more than 85% of export revenue.  

Morocco, situated in North Africa, has a more diversified economy, driven by agriculture, phosphates, and tourism. In 2022, Morocco’s GDP stood at approximately US$130 billion, with an annual inflation rate increasing from 1.9% in 2018 to 4.1% in 2022. The country has invested in renewable energy projects to reduce carbon emissions and has also sought to expand its industrial base, particularly in automobile manufacturing.  

Text B — The Trade Negotiations  

Discussions between Nigeria and Morocco gained momentum in 2021, focusing on developing a new free trade agreement (FTA). Both governments aim to remove or significantly lower tariffs on key exports, foster foreign direct investment (FDI), and align the agreement with Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). Nigeria, holding an absolute advantage, seeks to reduce tariffs on its crude oil, natural gas, and agricultural goods, while Morocco looks to expand exports of phosphate fertilizers, textiles, and automotive components.  

Negotiators also emphasize cooperation in energy, including the possibility of jointly developing a pipeline to carry Nigerian natural gas to Morocco’s Atlantic coast. Leaders from both countries hope this infrastructure project will attract global investors, boost regional trade, and enhance energy security in North and West Africa. Additionally, they intend to facilitate technology transfers and investment in Nigeria’s downstream refining sector, mitigating the region’s vulnerability to volatile oil prices.  

Text C — Impact on Local Industries and SDGs  

Several domestic industries in both countries have expressed concerns about increased foreign competition. In Nigeria, local manufacturing firms fear that cheaper imports from Morocco could diminish their market share. Meanwhile, Moroccan phosphate producers worry about potential drop-offs in demand if the global market shifts toward renewables and reduces fertilizer consumption. Stakeholders argue that, to remain competitive, companies must innovate and improve production efficiencies.  

Both Nigeria and Morocco see the FTA as an opportunity to address issues aligned with the SDGs. Officials highlight SDG 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy), SDG 8 (Decent Work and Economic Growth), and SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure) as key areas of focus. In Morocco, solar and wind power already account for over 36% of energy generation, and Nigeria aspires to expand its renewable capacity from the current 2% of the energy mix to 10% by 2030. Policymakers believe that increased trade and shared technological expertise could hasten the transition to cleaner energy.  

Trade liberalization could contribute to higher economic growth, but it also raises questions about equity. Nigeria’s rural populations depend heavily on agriculture, and the removal of tariffs might expose local farmers to intense foreign competition. In Morocco, labor unions have expressed concerns that cheaper agricultural imports from Nigeria could increase unemployment in the country’s farming regions. Both governments plan to continue negotiating social safeguards, vocational training programs, and targeted subsidies to protect vulnerable groups.

**Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators, 2022**
| Indicator                           | Nigeria | Morocco |
| ----------------------------------- | ------- | ------- |
| Nominal GDP (US\$ billions)         | 450     | 130     |
| Inflation Rate (%)                  | 16.9    | 4.1     |
| Unemployment Rate (%)               | 14.2    | 10.5    |
| Currency Exchange Rate (local/US\$) | 415.0   | 10.0    |


**Table 2: Proposed Tariff Reductions and FDI Projections**  

| Sector                | Proposed Tariff Reduction (%) | Projected FDI Inflows (US\$ millions) |
| --------------------- | ----------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Crude Oil (Nigeria)   | 10 → 0                        | 450 → 600                             |
| Phosphate Fertilizers | 12 → 3                        | 270 → 350                             |
| Textiles              | 15 → 5                        | 190 → 250                             |
| Automotive Components | 20 → 8                        | 300 → 400                             |

parts.content: Define the term inflation indicated in bold (Text A, paragraph 1).
parts.markscheme: Inflation is a sustained increase in the general price level of goods and services in an economy over a period of time.

parts.content: Define the term absolute advantage indicated in bold (Text B, paragraph 3).
parts.markscheme: Absolute advantage occurs when one country can produce a good using fewer resources (or more efficiently) than another country.

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the approximate difference in nominal GDP per capita between Nigeria and Morocco.
parts.markscheme:
- Nigeria’s nominal GDP = US$450 billion; 
- Morocco’s nominal GDP = US$130 billion.  
- Nigeria’s population = ~210 million; Morocco’s population = ~37 million (assuming ~37 million to reflect typical approximate population data for Morocco).  
- Nigeria’s GDP per capita ≈ 450 billion ÷ 210 million = US$2,143.  
- Morocco’s GDP per capita ≈ 130 billion ÷ 37 million = US$3,514.  
- Approximate difference ≈ US\$3,514- US\$2,143 = US\$1,371

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how Morocco’s investment in renewable energy (Text C, paragraph 6) could impact its long-run economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/6e9501f8-0038-42c6-b8b1-4fec6515997c)

- The investment in renewable energy increases Morocco’s productive capacity and shifts its long-run aggregate supply (LRAS) curve to the right as capital and technology improve.  
- In the short run, an increase in investment spending shifts aggregate demand (AD) to the right, boosting real output. In the long run, improved infrastructure and technology raise productive efficiency.  
- The result is a higher potential output (rightward shift of LRAS), leading to long-run economic growth.  
- This can be shown on an AD/AS diagram where AD shifts right (from AD1 to AD2) and LRAS shifts right (from LRAS1 to LRAS2), increasing real GDP in the long term.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 
<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using an exchange rate diagram, explain the likely effect on Nigeria’s currency if foreign demand for Nigerian crude oil significantly increases (Text C, paragraph 5).
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/d5c3e891-5e15-414c-887c-6f02bf033ad3)

- An increase in foreign demand for Nigerian crude oil raises export revenue and leads to higher demand for Nigeria’s currency in order to purchase Nigerian oil.  
- On a currency market diagram for the naira (Nigerian currency), the demand curve shifts right, causing an appreciation of the currency.  
- An appreciated currency makes imports cheaper for Nigerian consumers and might reduce the competitiveness of other exports (due to a higher exchange rate).  
- However, it may also help lower inflationary pressures by making imported goods less expensive.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 
<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using an externalities diagram, explain how increased adoption of renewable energy could address negative externalities associated with fossil fuels in Nigeria (Text C, paragraph 6).
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c027463c-2a8c-482f-b365-ff885c3a3015)

- Fossil fuels generate negative externalities, meaning the social cost (MSC) exceeds the private cost (MPC).  
- By increasing renewable energy, the quantity of fossil fuel use may fall, reducing the divergence between private and social costs and decreasing welfare loss.  
- The externalities diagram would show a shift in demand away from fossil fuels or a shift in supply (if considering a reduced external cost), moving closer to the socially optimal output where MSC = MSB.  
- This helps address environmental concerns (fewer carbon emissions) and promotes more sustainable energy consumption.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 
<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from the text/data (including Table 2) and your knowledge of economics, discuss the potential impact of the proposed Nigeria–Morocco trade agreement on both countries meeting two sustainable development goals.
parts.markscheme:
**Definition:**

- Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs): International objectives aimed at achieving sustainable economic growth, social inclusion, and environmental conservation.  
- Trade agreement: The removal or reduction of trade barriers to facilitate increased exchange of goods, services, and investment flows.

**Range of Arguments:**

- Impact on SDG 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy)  
  - The agreement includes potential joint development of infrastructure projects such as the natural gas pipeline (Text B, paragraph 4). This may improve energy access in both regions, making energy more affordable.  
  - Shared technological expertise in renewables (Text C, paragraph 6) accelerates the transition to cleaner energy, reducing emissions and diversifying the energy mix.  
  - Nigeria’s target to increase renewables from 2% to 10% by 2030 might be more achievable if technology and investment flows from Morocco are realized.  
  - Potential limitation: High implementation costs and dependence on global energy markets might restrict short-term gains in affordable energy.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/610a5c41-f44c-433e-bdd1-450c4d498bcd)

- Impact on SDG 8 (Decent Work and Economic Growth)  
  - Removal of tariffs and trade barriers (Table 2) encourages greater FDI, potentially creating jobs in both nations. For instance, automotive components FDI in Morocco and new refining investments in Nigeria (Text C, paragraph 5, Table 2) could expand employment.  
  - Trade liberalization may increase GDP growth through specialisation; Nigeria exporting oil and agricultural products, Morocco exporting phosphates and automotive parts.  
  - Limitations arise from possible displacement of workers in vulnerable industries. Nigerian manufacturing or Moroccan agriculture may face pressure if they cannot compete. Social safeguards and vocational training (Text C, paragraph 7) become crucial to prevent unemployment.

- Impact on SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure)  
  - The pipeline project (Text B, paragraph 4) enhances regional infrastructure, incentivizes industrial expansion.  
  - Investment in downstream refining in Nigeria fosters technology transfer and diversifies industrial base, reducing over-reliance on crude oil.  
  - Constraints emerge from large initial investment costs and potential policy uncertainties that may slow innovation.

- Positive Effects  
  - Table 2 shows projected FDI inflows (e.g., crude oil US 600 million, phosphate US 350 million), supporting industrialization and potential job creation.  
  - Environmental aspects are partly addressed by technology sharing in renewable energy, fulfilling green infrastructure investments.  
  - Larger market size through reduced tariffs might stimulate private sector growth, raising incomes and contributing to poverty reduction.

- Negative Effects  
  - Inequality might rise if rural farmers in Nigeria and small-scale producers in Morocco cannot match foreign efficiencies. This could jeopardize inclusive development goals.  
  - There may be a risk of reduced tariff revenues, potentially constraining government funds for public services that support SDGs.  
  - Volatile oil prices threaten stability, and dependence on single export commodities can hamper sustainable growth.

**Conclusion:**

- The trade agreement can promote SDG 7 and SDG 8 (and possibly SDG 9) by fostering cleaner energy, expanding employment, and enhancing industrial infrastructure.  
- However, challenges include uneven beneficiary distribution (potentially harming some local industries and rural populations), uncertain commodity prices, and high transitional costs.  
- Policymakers would need to address these issues through targeted subsidies, training programs, and long-term diversification measures to ensure the agreement significantly contributes to sustainable development in both countries.

questions.specification:
Cuba is an island nation in the Caribbean with a long history of state-led economic policies and a continued trade embargo imposed by the United States since 1960. The Cuban economy is heavily dependent on two key sectors: sugar and tourism. Sugar has traditionally been Cuba’s largest export, accounting for over 70% of export revenues, although inefficiencies and outdated technology have constrained growth in output. Meanwhile, tourism has become an increasingly important source of foreign exchange, representing nearly 24% of the nation’s gross domestic product (GDP). However, environmental concerns have arisen from rapid growth in visitor arrivals, including water pollution and congestion in popular coastal areas.

Cuba’s real GDP grew modestly from 2021 to 2022, although high inflation and global supply chain disruptions presented serious challenges. The government has attempted some economic reforms, including allowing limited private enterprise and trying to attract modest amounts of foreign direct investment (FDI). Nevertheless, significant barriers to trade remain in place due to the decades-long embargo, as do restrictions on access to many imported goods.

The Cuban government operates a progressive tax system to finance public healthcare, education, and subsidized food programmes. Alongside this, it has announced a new subsidy plan to boost sugar production and modernize processing facilities. Policymakers hope that these measures will generate both export revenues and improved economic prospects.

Below are selected data from the Cuban economy:

Table 1: Key Macroeconomic Indicators for Cuba (2021–2022)

| Year | Real GDP (US$ billion) | Population (millions) | Inflation (%) | Government Injection (US$ million) | Marginal Propensity to Consume (MPC) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2021 | 54.0 | 11.28 | 12.5 | 500 | 0.80 |
| 2022 | 58.3 | 11.29 | 10.2 | 500 | 0.80 |

Table 2: Demand for Sugar in Cuba

| Price (US$ per tonne) | Quantity Demanded (tonnes) |
| --- | --- |
| 380 | 3 200 000 |
| 410 | 2 800 000 |

Table 3: Tourism in Cuba

| Year | Total Tourism Receipts (US$ billion) | Tourism as % of GDP | International Visitors (millions) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2021 | 3.5 | 22 | 2.1 |
| 2022 | 4.2 | 24 | 2.6 |

Table 4: Selected Cuban Tax Rates

| Type of Tax                         | Rate             |
| ----------------------------------- | ---------------- |
| Corporate Income Tax                | 30%              |
| Personal Income Tax (Progressive)   | Top bracket 35%  |
| Indirect Taxes (various categories) | 0%, 10%, or 15%  |

– A policy option is to diversify export production beyond sugar.

  – This approach can be supported by targeted government spending on research and development in agriculture (e.g., coffee or citrus) and new manufacturing sectors, using the progressive tax revenue to fund such initiatives.  
  – The case study suggests significant reliance on sugar (over 70% of export revenues); lowering this concentration could buffer Cuba against global price drops and negative shocks.  
  – Increased FDI incentives, combined with reduced trade barriers (where possible), can help shift production into more technologically advanced sectors and services.  
  – Tourism’s share of GDP is also large at 24%; encouraging ecotourism and more sustainable visitor management could supplement the traditional tourist segment and reduce environmental harm.  
  – A policy of diversification could protect employment levels by creating job opportunities in new sectors, mitigating potential sugar industry downturns.  
  – Opportunity costs exist for the government, as funds invested in diversifying exports might be diverted from other public services, yet long-term economic resilience may outweigh short-term spending trade-offs.  
  – Reduced dependence on sugar exports can stabilize export revenues, improving external accounts and resilience to global market uncertainties.  
  – This policy can reduce the negative impact of sugar export reliance while promoting sustainable growth in other parts of the economy.
