parts.content: Using the information in Table 1, calculate Vietnam's terms of trade for the year 2022. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
• Terms of Trade (ToT) = (Index of Export Prices ÷ Index of Import Prices) × 100
• ToT (2022) = ($112 \div 120$) × 100 = 93.33.
• Final answer: 93.33.

parts.content: The government’s planned investment in high-tech industrial parks for 2023 is US$20 billion, and the MPC is estimated at 0.60. Using the Keynesian multiplier formula, calculate the total potential increase in national income resulting from this investment. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
• Multiplier ($k$) = $1 \div (1 - MPC) = 1 \div (1 - 0.60) = 2.5$.
• Change in National Income = $Investment \times k = US$20 \text{ billion} \times 2.5 = US$50 \text{ billion}$.
• Final answer: US$50 billion.

parts.content: Using the data in Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for smartphones when the price increases from US$400 to US$480. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
• % Change in Quantity Demanded = $[(10 - 12) \div 12] \times 100 = -16.67%$.
• % Change in Price = $[(480 - 400) \div 400] \times 100 = 20%$.
• PED = $-16.67% \div 20% = -0.833$ (or $0.833$).
• Final answer: -0.833 (or 0.833).

parts.content: Using the data in Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of supply (PES) for smartphones when the price increases from US$400 to US$480. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
• % Change in Quantity Supplied = $[(11 - 8) \div 8] \times 100 = 37.5%$.
• % Change in Price = $[(480 - 400) \div 400] \times 100 = 20%$.
• PES = $37.5% \div 20% = 1.875$.
• Final answer: 1.875.

parts.content: Define the term “absolute advantage.”
parts.markscheme: • Absolute advantage refers to the ability of a country to produce a greater quantity of a good or service than competitors using the same amount of resources.

parts.content: Using a demand and supply diagram for an exporting nation, explain how international trade generates a net welfare gain for a country like Vietnam in the electronics market.
parts.markscheme:
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-chemistry-new-662c292d-bd84-4eef-b2bd-758d81d856b7-1771938518510/01534808-8027-45f2-9548-fb6b0e5de595.jpeg)
• When a country opens to trade and the world price ($P_w$) is higher than the domestic price, it will export the surplus $(Q_s - Q_d)$.
• Domestic producer surplus increases significantly as they sell more at a higher price.
• While domestic consumer surplus falls, the gain to producers exceeds the loss to consumers.
• The net welfare gain is the area of the triangle formed between the domestic demand and supply curves and the world price line.

<marks>Award 2 marks for a correctly labeled diagram showing domestic supply/demand, world price above equilibrium, and the net welfare gain triangle.</marks>
<marks>Award 2 marks for an explanation of the changes in producer/consumer surplus leading to a net gain.</marks>

parts.content: Using information from Table 3, calculate the percentage of Vietnam's total export revenue that was generated by the Electronics sector in 2022. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
• (Revenue from Electronics ÷ Total Export Revenue) × 100
• ($110 \div 370$) × 100 = 29.73%
• Final answer: 29.73%.

parts.content: Explain how international trade can allow Vietnamese firms to benefit from “economies of scale.”
parts.markscheme:
• International trade provides access to global markets, allowing Vietnamese firms to produce far larger volumes than the domestic market would support.
• Increased production levels allow firms to spread fixed costs (such as R&D or machinery) over a larger output, lowering the average cost per unit.
• Bulk-buying of raw materials and the implementation of specialized production lines become more feasible at higher outputs.
• This reduction in long-run average costs improves the international competitiveness of Vietnamese exports.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy that the Vietnamese government could implement to move the economy from low-value assembly toward higher-value technological exports.
parts.markscheme:
• Vietnam currently relies heavily on assembly (as seen in Table 3's high Electronics revenue but dependence on imported machinery).
• A recommended policy is a supply-side investment in human capital and R&D (e.g., US$20 billion in high-tech zones mentioned in text).
• Investment in education and technical training increases labor productivity, allowing workers to perform complex innovation rather than simple assembly.
• Higher productivity shifts the Long-Run Aggregate Supply (LRAS) curve to the right, as shown in the diagram below, leading to higher potential real output and improved international competitiveness.
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-chemistry-new-662c292d-bd84-4eef-b2bd-758d81d856b7-1771938518510/063e21a1-1d48-458a-9175-65032293b0a4.jpeg)
• The multiplier effect of the US$20 billion investment (calculated as 2.5 in part 1) would generate a US$50 billion increase in national income, providing more tax revenue for further infrastructure.
• Moving to higher-value exports improves the Terms of Trade (Table 1 shows ToT at 93.33, suggesting imports are currently relatively expensive; higher-value exports would increase the Export Price Index).
• This reduces vulnerability to global input cost shocks and increases the value-added component of domestic production.
• Recommendation: The government should specifically target R&D subsidies and high-tech vocational training to foster local innovation and reduce the current reliance on low-margin assembly.

<marks>Award up to 4 marks for identifying and explaining a relevant policy (e.g., R&D, education) with reference to the text.</marks>
<marks>Award up to 3 marks for linking the policy to economic indicators (ToT, multiplier, value-added).</marks>
<marks>Award up to 3 marks for a clear recommendation supported by economic reasoning and diagrams.</marks>

questions.specification:
**Chilean economy faces downturn as copper prices slide**

Chile's economy, the world's leading copper producer, is experiencing significant pressure due to falling global commodity prices and a weakening currency. Copper, accounting for over half of Chile's export earnings, has seen its market price drop by more than 40% between 2011 and 2015. This decline has been driven primarily by cooling demand from China—the largest consumer of Chilean copper—and increased production from competing mines in Peru and Africa.

The Chilean Peso (CLP) has depreciated by 15% against the US dollar (USD) during this period. While a weaker currency typically boosts export competitiveness, it has simultaneously increased the cost of essential imports, contributing to a widening current account deficit. Profitability for state-owned and private mining firms has also plummeted, reducing government tax revenues.

In June 2015, Chile recorded a monthly trade deficit of USD 1.5 billion, a sharp increase from the USD 0.8 billion deficit reported for the same month the previous year. This growing imbalance has raised concerns about long-term financial stability and sustainable economic growth.

The impact on national wealth is evident in the GDP data. Real GDP growth in Chile slowed to 1.8% in 2014, down from 4.5% in 2013. Analysts estimate that the collapse in copper prices alone reduced export revenues by a value equivalent to 3% of Chile's total GDP in late 2014.

Faced with these challenges, Chilean policymakers and economists are debating further interest rate cuts by the Central Bank of Chile. Advocates for monetary easing suggest that stimulus is necessary to support domestic demand and help non-mining sectors, such as agriculture and salmon exports, which have struggled to fill the gap left by the mining slowdown.

Chile has worked to diversify its economy, particularly in high-value agricultural products and wine. However, the global economic slowdown and the volatility of the peso have created a difficult environment for these emerging export sectors.

Table 1: Chile's Trade Data (in USD billion)

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Year} & \textbf{Exports} & \textbf{Imports} \\ \hline
2013 & 76.5 & 77.3 \\ \hline
2014 & 74.2 & 77.6 \\ \hline
\end{array}
$$

Table 2: Chile's GDP Growth and Trade Balance (2013-2014)

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Year} & \textbf{GDP Growth (\%)} & \textbf{Trade Deficit (USD billion)} \\ \hline
2013 & 4.5 & 0.8 \\ \hline
2014 & 1.8 & 3.4 \\ \hline
\end{array}
$$

parts.content: Define the term current account.
parts.markscheme:
A component of the balance of payments which records the value of exports and imports of goods and services, <marks>1 mark</marks>

as well as primary income (net income flows) and secondary income (current transfers). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: List two reasons why copper prices may have fallen.
parts.markscheme:
- Cooling or slowing demand from China, the world's largest consumer, reduced the overall market demand for copper. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increased global production and supply from competing regions (such as Peru or Africa) led to a market surplus. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Global economic uncertainty led to reduced industrial activity, further lowering the demand for raw materials like copper. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>Award max 2 marks</marks>

parts.content: Draw a demand and supply diagram to illustrate the fall in copper prices.
parts.markscheme:
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-6999ffba-91af-4973-a4f6-169d37dc1555-1771840759346/26ca91c0-2de9-4b98-8159-739576d3d50f.jpeg)

- The diagram correctly labels the axes (Price of Copper and Quantity of Copper) and shows the initial equilibrium. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- A rightward shift of the supply curve (from S1 to S2) representing increased global production AND/OR a leftward shift of the demand curve (from D1 to D2) representing slowing Chinese demand. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The new equilibrium clearly shows a lower price (from P1 to P2), reflecting the 40% price drop mentioned in the text. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using a PED diagram, explain how the price elasticity of demand for copper affects Chile's export revenue.
parts.markscheme:
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-6999ffba-91af-4973-a4f6-169d37dc1555-1771840759346/e1707c44-8b86-4c81-bb4f-4550194f4412.jpeg)

- Commodities like copper typically have price inelastic demand (PED < 1) because they are essential raw materials with few immediate substitutes in industrial processes. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In the diagram, the steep demand curve shows that a 40% fall in price leads to a much smaller percentage increase in quantity demanded. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Because the fall in price is proportionally greater than the increase in quantity, total export revenue (P × Q) falls significantly. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This explains why falling prices reduced Chile's export revenues by an amount equivalent to 3% of its GDP. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>Award max 4 marks for diagram and explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a PES diagram, explain how the price elasticity of supply of copper influences Chile's production levels.
parts.markscheme:
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-6999ffba-91af-4973-a4f6-169d37dc1555-1771840759346/4d799bec-33fd-4bac-b16d-fead2f0d8b11.jpeg)

- The price elasticity of supply (PES) for copper mining is generally inelastic in the short run because of high fixed costs, specialized equipment, and the long time required to open or close mines. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The diagram shows a steep supply curve where a large fall in price (P1 to P2) leads to a very small reduction in quantity supplied (Q1 to Q2). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Chilean firms continue to produce high volumes even as prices crash because they need to cover fixed costs and cannot easily shift resources. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This continued high production despite falling prices contributes to a global oversupply, further depressing the price of copper. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>Award max 4 marks for diagram and explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a market structure diagram, explain how the oligopolistic nature of the mining sector impacts competition in the industry.
parts.markscheme:
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-6999ffba-91af-4973-a4f6-169d37dc1555-1771840759346/f4484cd0-c1bc-4f63-85de-47d45b3df3f8.jpeg)

- The copper mining industry is often an oligopoly dominated by a few large state-owned (e.g., Codelco) and multinational firms due to extreme barriers to entry (capital intensity). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The diagram shows the firm producing at MR=MC, charging a price P that is above Average Cost (AC), resulting in abnormal (supernormal) profits. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- These firms have significant market power and are interdependent; actions by one producer to increase output (to maintain total revenue) can trigger similar actions by others, leading to price wars or market gluts. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The high barriers to entry prevent new competitors from entering when profits are high, but also mean supply doesn't exit quickly when prices fall. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>Award max 4 marks for diagram and explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using an AD-AS diagram, explain the impact of falling copper prices on Chile's economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-6999ffba-91af-4973-a4f6-169d37dc1555-1771840759346/bdccee63-1ce9-4f77-bcb4-d929265fc336.jpeg)

- Copper exports are a major component of Chile's Aggregate Demand ($$AD = C + I + G + (X - M)$$). Falling copper prices reduce export revenue (X), causing a leftward shift in the AD curve from AD1 to AD2. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This shift leads to a lower equilibrium level of real output (from Y1 to Y2), directly reflecting the slowdown in GDP growth (from 4.5% to 1.8%). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Additionally, lower mining profits reduce corporate investment (I) and government spending (G) due to lower tax receipts, further shifting AD to the left. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The diagram shows the economy moving to a new equilibrium with lower real GDP and a lower price level/inflation rate. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>Award max 4 marks for diagram and explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the potential effects of further interest rate cuts on Chile's economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### **Definitions**
- **Interest Rate**: The cost of borrowing money or the reward for saving, determined by the central bank.
- **Economic Growth**: An increase in the production of goods and services in an economy, measured by the percentage change in real GDP.
- **Monetary Policy**: The use of interest rates and other monetary tools by the central bank to influence the level of aggregate demand.

### **Potential Positive Effects on Growth**
- **Stimulating Consumption and Investment**: Lower interest rates reduce the cost of borrowing for households (boosting C) and firms (boosting I). This is particularly important for diversifying the economy into non-mining sectors like agriculture and manufacturing. 
- **Exchange Rate Effects**: Rate cuts usually lead to a further depreciation of the CLP as investors seek higher returns elsewhere. A weaker peso makes Chilean agricultural and wine exports cheaper on the global market, potentially increasing net exports (X-M).
- **Counteracting the AD Shift**: Monetary stimulus can shift the AD curve back to the right, helping to recover the growth lost from the copper price crash (from 1.8% back toward previous levels).
- **Multiplier Effect**: Increased spending in non-mining sectors can lead to job creation and further rounds of spending, boosting the overall economy.

### **Potential Negative Effects and Limitations**
- **Inflationary Pressures**: A depreciating currency makes imported goods (like fuel and technology) more expensive, which could lead to cost-push inflation, reducing consumers' real purchasing power.
- **Wide Trade Deficit**: If Chile relies heavily on imported capital goods for its diversification efforts, the higher cost of imports (due to the weaker CLP) could worsen the trade deficit, which already rose to USD 3.4 billion in 2014.
- **Confidence Issues**: If businesses and consumers are pessimistic about the global economy (especially China's slowdown), they may not borrow or spend even if interest rates are low (the "liquidity trap" or low confidence problem).
- **Debt Burdens**: If Chilean firms have debt denominated in USD, a depreciating peso makes that debt much harder to service, potentially leading to bankruptcies and reduced investment.

### **Evaluation**
- **Dependence on External Factors**: The effectiveness of Chilean interest rate cuts depends largely on China. If Chinese demand for copper remains stagnant, monetary policy may only provide a temporary floor for the economy rather than a full recovery.
- **Time Lags**: Monetary policy has significant time lags (often 12-18 months) before the full effects on GDP are felt.
- **Sectoral Shift**: While rate cuts help non-mining sectors, these sectors may not be large enough to offset the massive 3% GDP loss from copper revenue in the short term.
- **Conclusion**: Further interest rate cuts are likely necessary to prevent a recession, but they must be managed carefully to avoid an inflationary spiral from the depreciating currency. Long-term growth will likely require structural reforms to reduce the over-reliance on a single commodity.

questions.specification:
## Recent Economic Developments in Israel

Israel, located in the Middle East along the Mediterranean Sea, has built a reputation as a high-tech powerhouse, with technology exports accounting for almost 40% of total exports in 2021. Despite its advanced economic status, certain segments of the population still face persistent poverty, particularly among ultra-Orthodox communities and some minorities. In 2020, the official poverty rate stood at 20.4%, prompting the government to expand targeted social assistance programs. At the same time, policymakers continue to grapple with balancing macroeconomic stability, fostering innovation, and ensuring inclusive growth.

From 2018 to 2021, Israel’s real GDP growth was highly influenced by both global economic cycles and domestic factors such as rising consumption, strong export performance in the high-tech sector, and fluctuating tourism revenues. Periodic geopolitical tensions introduced volatility in foreign direct investment (FDI), as did global shocks that affected investor confidence. Meanwhile, the Bank of Israel generally maintained an accommodative monetary policy to counter moderate inflation pressures, which averaged around 1% over this period—slightly below the mid-point of the central bank’s target range.

A major source of economic concern for Israel is structural unemployment, especially for workers lacking the skills needed in technology-driven industries. In specific regions, poor infrastructure and limited transportation links further hinder access to higher-paying jobs, reinforcing what some analysts describe as a local poverty trap. To address these challenges, government initiatives include investing in education and vocational training, especially in areas with higher concentrations of low-income populations. Beyond domestic considerations, Israel is party to various free trade agreements with key markets such as the United States and the European Union. These agreements have reduced tariff barriers and spurred export growth, although domestic producers in traditional manufacturing sectors sometimes struggle to remain competitive.

The government’s budget deficit has oscillated in recent years. In 2020, increased spending to support households and businesses during the global economic downturn pushed the fiscal deficit above 5% of GDP. Nonetheless, Israel’s current account balance remained in surplus, aided by robust exports in software, pharmaceuticals, and defense technology. Concurrently, the Israeli new shekel (ILS) exhibited a slight trend toward appreciation, partly due to a steady flow of incoming FDI in technology start-ups.

Despite the nation’s strong high-tech profile, around 14% of the working-age population remains in low-paid service jobs. Many among these workers lack the qualifications necessary to transition into more lucrative sectors. Poverty rates also remain stubbornly high in certain demographic groups, underscoring the potential for a “poverty cycle”, in which limited education and weak earning potential perpetuate lower living standards across generations. Critics argue that the government must undertake more targeted social spending and refine its labor market policies to break the cycle of intergenerational poverty.

In the international arena, trade ties with both developed and emerging markets continue to evolve. Israel has considered further reductions in non-tariff barriers as well as new bilateral agreements to diversify export destinations beyond traditional partners. Supporters of these measures point to larger consumer markets for Israeli products, while critics worry about the competitiveness of domestic industries that may not be ready to face unfettered global competition.

Israel’s technology-driven growth strategy has indeed lifted per capita income above US$47,000 in 2021, but it has also accentuated regional income disparities. To leverage opportunities for sustainable development, policymakers are exploring enhanced public–private partnerships targeting infrastructure in peripheral regions, alongside educational reforms that promote STEM and vocational skills. Government officials argue that strategic policies to foster technological innovation, matched with effective social programs, could bolster productivity and reduce poverty in the longer term.

Below are two tables summarizing key macroeconomic and development indicators from 2018 to 2021:

**Table 1: Israel’s Selected Macroeconomic Indicators (2018–2021)**

| Indicator                        | 2018   | 2019   | 2020   | 2021   |
|----------------------------------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| Nominal GDP (US$ billion)        | 355    | 394    | 395    | 480    |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)         | 3.6    | 3.5    | -2.2   | 8.1    |
| Inflation Rate (%)               | 0.8    | 0.3    | -0.7   | 2.4    |
| Unemployment Rate (%)            | 4.0    | 3.8    | 6.1    | 5.2    |
| Budget Deficit (% of GDP)        | -2.9   | -3.7   | -5.2   | -4.5   |
| Current Account Balance (% GDP)  | 3.3    | 3.7    | 3.5    | 4.2    |
| Exchange Rate (ILS per US$)      | 3.63   | 3.48   | 3.35   | 3.20   |

**Table 2: Selected Poverty and Development Indicators**

| Indicator                                           | 2018  | 2019  | 2020  | 2021  |
|-----------------------------------------------------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| Poverty Rate (%)                                    | 21.0  | 20.7  | 20.4  | 20.4  |

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the extent to which Israel’s technology-based growth strategy can reduce poverty and income inequality in the long term.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 

**Definition:**

- Poverty: a condition where individuals lack sufficient income or resources to meet basic needs.  
- Income inequality: the uneven distribution of income across different segments of a population.  
- Technology-based growth strategy: an economic growth model that prioritizes innovation, high-tech industries, and skilled labor to drive expansion in output and exports.

**Range of Arguments:**

  1. Enhanced High-Wage Employment Opportunities  
     - The high-tech sector can generate well-paying jobs, driving average annual wages higher (from US$39,500 in 2020 to US$45,000 in 2021).  
     - Rising wages can lower poverty through higher disposable incomes, especially if workers transition from low-paid service jobs into the tech sector.  
      - However, a sizable share of the population (14% in low-paid jobs) lacks the requisite skills, which may sustain inequality if they do not gain access to training.

  2. Spillover Effects on Government Revenue and Social Spending  
     - Robust high-tech exports (nearly 40% of total exports) can increase tax revenues.  
     - Additional revenue could finance social assistance programs or public education, aiming to break intergenerational poverty cycles.  
     - Government willingness to direct these funds effectively is critical; expansion in social spending (e.g., education at around 9% of total budget in 2020) needs to sustain itself to narrow disparities.

  3. Potential for Widening the Income Gap  
     - Skilled workers benefit most from technology-driven growth, leading to an intensification of structural unemployment for under-skilled groups.  
     - More workers with high paying jobs, will increase income inequality and shift the Lorenz curve from L1 to L2. 
     - Persistent poverty among the ultra-Orthodox and certain minorities suggests that not everyone can participate equally in tech-based growth.  
     - Income gaps could widen if the high-tech sector remains centered in urban areas with better infrastructure, leaving peripheral regions behind.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/6d085ac3-b7f0-4222-a3d3-ff8fc90660a8)

  4. Importance of Complementary Policies  
     - Targeted job training, improvements in infrastructure, and educational reforms are vital to ensure inclusive growth.  
     - Israel’s strategy of forming public–private partnerships to invest in peripheral areas can provide better transportation links, encouraging more widespread participation in economic opportunities.  
     - Without robust policy support, technology-led expansion might keep certain communities trapped in cycles of poverty, as indicated by the stable poverty rate around 20.4%.

**Conclusion:**

  - A technology-based growth strategy can offer significant potential for raising incomes and reducing poverty, mainly through higher-value employment and stronger government revenues for social programs.  
  - Inequality reduction depends on inclusive policies, such as expanded education and vocational training, to ensure skill acquisition for groups currently left behind.  
  - Over the long term, sustained investment in human capital, infrastructure, and social services is critical for this strategy to translate into broad-based improvements in living standards and narrower income gaps.

parts.content: Explain how, according to the theory of absolute and comparative advantage, countries can benefit from free trade.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### Definitions:

- **Absolute advantage:** The ability of a country to produce a good using fewer resources than another country.

- **Comparative advantage:** The ability of a country to produce a good at a lower opportunity cost than another country.

- **Free trade:** The exchange of goods and services between countries without barriers such as tariffs, quotas, or other restrictions.

### Economic Theory:

- According to absolute advantage, a country should specialize in producing goods for which it can use fewer resources (or produce more output with the same resources) than other countries. This ensures higher efficiency in overall production.

- According to comparative advantage, a country should specialize in producing goods for which it has the lowest opportunity cost. This means that even if one country has an absolute advantage in producing multiple goods, it benefits more by focusing on the good it can produce relatively more efficiently, while importing goods that other countries produce at a lower opportunity cost.

- Specialization based on comparative advantage leads to higher total output across all trading nations. When each country concentrates on goods it can produce most efficiently, total world production increases.

- Free trade enables countries to exchange these specialized outputs. Without trade barriers, countries obtain goods they do not produce (or which they produce less efficiently) from foreign markets and export the goods in which they have a comparative advantage. This exchange raises global living standards by allowing nations to consume a greater variety and quantity of goods.

- Opportunity cost is central to the comparative advantage theory. Trading partners can consume outside their own production possibility frontiers (PPFs) if they specialize and trade. The differences in opportunity costs among countries create gains from trade.

- There are assumptions such as constant costs of production, perfect mobility of factors of production within each country, and no transport costs when illustrating the theory. In reality, some of these assumptions may not hold, but the principle still highlights potential efficiency and welfare gains from trade.

### Diagram:

- A typical PPF diagram can show two goods (for example, Good X and Good Y). One country has a lower opportunity cost in producing Good X, while the other opportunity cost in producing Good Y.

- After specialization, each country's production point shifts toward the axis representing the good it produces relatively more efficiently.

- With free trade, the consumption possibilities curve extends beyond each country's original PPF, illustrating how both countries can consume more of both goods through specialization and exchange.




![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/e57cb6a0-4e34-4678-9b83-9234b4b99aa7)

parts.content: Using real-world examples, evaluate the view that the theory of comparative advantage has no limitations.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### Definition

- **Comparative Advantage**: The ability of a country to produce a good or service at a lower opportunity cost than another country, allowing it to specialize in and export that product.  

### Economic Theory

- Comparative advantage arises when countries specialize in the production of goods where they sacrifice fewer alternative outputs than other countries.  
- Specialization according to comparative advantage should, in theory, lead to an efficient global allocation of resources and increased total output by allowing countries to focus on producing goods where they have a relative efficiency.  
- Lower domestic production costs relative to other countries create export opportunities because the goods become more price competitive, higher costs relative to other countries imply reliance on imports for those goods.  
- Free trade propelled by comparative advantage assumptions:  
  - No trade barriers (tariffs, quotas).  
  - Perfect mobility of factors of production (labor and capital).  
  - No transportation or transaction costs.  
  - Constant returns to scale and no externalities.  
- In practice, countries that specialize in goods for which they have a comparative advantage can experience an expansion of export industries, a reduction in domestic prices, and an improvement in resource allocation because resources are used more efficiently as efficiency and productivity increase.

### Diagram

- A pair of Production Possibility Frontiers (PPFs) can illustrate comparative advantage by showing two countries with different opportunity costs.  
- When each country specializes in the product with the lower opportunity cost, combined global output increases.  
- This diagram highlights how trade allows both countries to move beyond their individual PPFs.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/21622d04-fbf0-49f0-bc02-57ae65d229d1)

### Evaluation

- Short-run impacts:  
  - Positive: Specialization can foster export-led growth, reduced consumer prices, and access to foreign markets.  
  - Negative: Adjustment costs can be significant; industries without a comparative advantage may contract rapidly because they can't compete effectively, leading to short-run unemployment.  
- Long-run impacts:  
  - Positive: Higher production efficiency, greater global output, and increased consumer choice result from specialization and trade.  
  - Negative: Persistent deficits in certain industries, resource overreliance on a narrow export base, and vulnerabilities if global demand shifts. 
	
**Real World Examples**  

  - China has specialized in manufactured goods, offering relatively low labor costs. In 2018, China accounted for roughly 28% of global manufacturing output, illustrating strong export-led growth. However, the U.S. Rust Belt witnessed heavy job losses as manufacturing shifted abroad, showcasing structural unemployment.  
  - Bangladesh’s textiles and garments sectors, employing over 4 million workers, have grown rapidly due to a comparative advantage in low-cost labor. Still, poor infrastructure and reliance on a single export sector highlight vulnerabilities.  
  - Germany’s specialization in high-value engineering exports (e.g., automobiles, machinery) has fueled strong economic growth, but reliance on imported energy can strain trade balances and expose the economy to global energy price fluctuations.  

**Limitations:**  
  - Early stages of trade liberalization can create imbalances when factors of production are not perfectly mobile (e.g., workers cannot quickly shift industries).  
  - Trade barriers, political constraints, and technology gaps can distort true comparative advantages, limiting real-world efficiency gains.  
  - Environmental externalities (e.g., pollution from large-scale manufacturing) are not fully accounted for in the basic model and can impose societal costs because they affect public health and welfare.  
  - Persistent reliance on one industry may limit diversification, posing risks if international demand or resource availability changes. These constraints demonstrate the gap between theoretical outcomes and real-world complexities.

### Conclusion

- The theory of comparative advantage highlights significant potential benefits from trade, including efficiency gains and higher total output (Germany's engineering exports).  
- In reality, limitations such as trade barriers, factor immobility, externalities, and adjustment costs suggest that the theory is not universally flawless.  
- While comparative advantage remains a cornerstone of international trade theory, real-world evidence shows that it does have recognized constraints, and its effectiveness depends on how well actual conditions align with theoretical assumptions.

questions.specification:
## Brazil–South Africa Economic Partnership Agreement (BSEPA)

Brazil and South Africa are two emerging economies with diverse industrial bases and growing populations. In 2022, Brazil’s real gross domestic product (GDP) expanded by 3.8%, fueled primarily by agricultural exports and rising consumption. Meanwhile, South Africa recorded a 2.1% increase in real GDP, despite high unemployment and persistent income inequality. In both nations, inflation rates have been climbing; Brazil’s inflation rate reached 9.2% while South Africa’s hit 6.5%. Policymakers in both countries have been attempting to manage these challenges through a combination of fiscal policy and adjustments to interest rates while they pursue trade opportunities abroad.

In 2023, Brazil and South Africa signed the Brazil–South Africa Economic Partnership Agreement (BSEPA), aiming to lower tariffs on goods such as automobiles, agricultural products, and technology. This agreement is designed to address trade imbalances, promote growth, and intensify cooperation in sectors such as renewable energy. Brazil’s Minister of Finance highlighted the potential for increased foreign direct investment (FDI), especially as investors seek stable returns in infrastructure projects. South Africa also hopes to gain increased access to Brazil’s markets, particularly for its metals and other value-added products.

Despite these optimistic goals, there are concerns about structural weaknesses in both economies. In South Africa, high youth unemployment and labor market rigidity may limit the capacity to benefit from new export opportunities. Brazil, meanwhile, has struggled with political uncertainty, which has affected consumer and investor confidence. Both governments are particularly interested in developing small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) through preferential export credits and targeted subsidies. Initial negotiations pointed to a potential surge in bilateral trade from US 3.5 billion in 2021 to an estimated US$5.2 billion by 2025. However, some economists warn that currency fluctuations and shifts in global commodity prices could undermine these projections.

The BSEPA includes provisions to encourage the sustainable use of natural resources. Brazil has a comparative advantage in agriculture, especially in coffee and soybeans, while South Africa focuses on minerals and manufactured goods. Environmental groups have flagged concerns that the expansion of export agriculture might exacerbate deforestation in Brazil, which already lost 4 million hectares of forest cover between 2016 and 2021. In South Africa, environmentalists worry about the external costs of mining, such as pollution and degradation of water sources, which are not reflected in market prices.

Additionally, the agreement seeks to deepen cooperation in services, including tourism, transport, and financial services. Brazil hopes to attract more South African tourists by relaxing visa requirements, potentially increasing tourist arrivals by 25% within two years. South Africa aims to strengthen its financial services sector through partnerships with Brazilian banks, introducing more efficient digital payment systems. Both governments also anticipate that deregulation in certain areas could lead to short-term structural unemployment if local firms cannot compete, emphasizing the need for retraining programs.

As part of the BSEPA negotiations, each country agreed to set up a Stabilization Fund meant to reduce the impact of currency volatility on trade. In the last five years, the Brazilian real has depreciated by 20% against the US dollar, while the South African rand has lost 15% of its value, making import prices higher and complicating inflation control. Officials from both nations hope that coordinated monetary policies and strategic interventions in foreign exchange markets can provide greater predictability for businesses.

**Table 1: Selected Economic Indicators (2022)**

| Indicator                      | Brazil  | South Africa |
| -----------------------------  | ------- | ------------ |
| Real GDP growth rate (%)       | 3.8     | 2.1          |
| Inflation rate (%)             | 9.2     | 6.5          |
| Unemployment rate (%)          | 11.3    | 32.6         |
| Interest rate (%)              | 9.5     | 5.0          |
| FDI inflows (US$ billions)     | 46.0    | 4.8          |

**Table 2: Bilateral Trade Data (2021)**

| Trade Flow                       | Value (US$ billions) |
| -------------------------------  | -------------------- |
| Brazil’s exports to South Africa | 2.0                  |
| South Africa’s exports to Brazil | 1.5                  |
| Projected total trade (2025)     | 5.2                  |

parts.content: Define the term unemployment mentioned in the text (Text A, paragraph 1).
parts.markscheme: When people of working age (16-65) who are actively seeking and able to work, but are not employed.

parts.content: Define the term foreign direct investment (FDI) mentioned in the text (Text A, paragraph 2).
parts.markscheme: A FDI, is an investment by a foreigner into another countries where the investor buy at least 10% of e.g a business.

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the approximate difference (in percentage points) between Brazil’s and South Africa’s inflation rates in 2022.
parts.markscheme:
Calculation of inflation rate difference  
Brazil’s inflation rate (9.2%) - South Africa’s inflation rate (6.5%) = 2.7 percentage points (approximate difference).

parts.content: Sketch a supply and demand diagram to show what might happen to the market for South African manufactured goods if domestic unemployment remains high and local consumers reduce their spending (Table 1).
parts.markscheme:
- Market for South African manufactured goods (high unemployment and reduced consumer spending)  
- A decrease in consumer spending will shift the demand curve leftwards in the market for domestically produced manufactured goods, resulting in a lower equilibrium price and quantity if supply remains unchanged. Firms may reduce output and lay off workers, potentially exacerbating unemployment.

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how rising interest rates (Table 1) could affect real GDP in Brazil.
parts.markscheme:
- Rising interest rates in Brazil and effect on real GDP (AD/AS diagram)  
Higher interest rates can increase the cost of borrowing for consumers and firms, potentially reducing consumption and investment. This reduction in consumption and investment shifts aggregate demand (AD) to the left. A leftward shift of AD lowers the price level and decreases real GDP in the short run. 
- A fall in real GDP can slow economic growth and possibly worsen unemployment if firms reduce production in response to the lower level of aggregate demand.

parts.content: Using an exchange rate diagram, explain how a significant depreciation of the Brazilian real might affect the volume of its exports to South Africa (Text A, paragraph 6).
parts.markscheme:
- Depreciation of the Brazilian real and exports to South Africa (exchange rate diagram)  
A significant depreciation of the Brazilian real lowers the price of Brazilian goods in terms of South African rand, making Brazilian exports relatively cheaper for South African buyers. This is likely to increase the volume of Brazilian exports to South Africa, ceteris paribus.
- In an exchange rate diagram, an increase in the supply of the real or decrease in demand for the real shifts the equilibrium to a lower exchange rate, stimulating export demand.

parts.content: Using an international trade diagram for South Africa, explain how lowering tariffs on agricultural products might influence the equilibrium price and quantity of Brazilian soybeans traded with South Africa (Text A, paragraph 2).
parts.markscheme:
- Lowering tariffs on agricultural products in South Africa (international trade diagram)  
When South Africa reduces tariffs on Brazilian soybeans, the domestic price of imports decreases. 
- On an international trade diagram, the world supply curve becomes more significant at a lower price to domestic consumers, leading to a reduction in the domestic market price and an increase in the quantity of soybeans imported from Brazil. 
- Consumers gain from lower prices, while domestic producers may receive a lower price and produce fewer soybeans, suggesting a redistribution of welfare from domestic producers to consumers.

parts.content: Using an externalities diagram, explain how environmental damage from mining in South Africa (Text A, paragraph 4) can lead to market failure.
parts.markscheme:
- Environmental damage from mining in South Africa (externalities diagram)  
Mining generates negative externalities, such as pollution and water degradation, creating a divergence between marginal private costs (MPC) and marginal social costs (MSC). 
- The social cost of production exceeds the private cost, causing an overproduction of the good from society’s perspective. In an externalities diagram, the mining industry’s equilibrium output is higher than the socially optimal output, leading to a welfare loss and market failure because the external costs are not reflected in the market price.

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the potential impact of the Brazil–South Africa Economic Partnership Agreement on economic growth and development in both countries.
parts.markscheme:
**Definition:**  

- Economic growth: An increase in real GDP over time.  
- Economic development: A broad measure of welfare, involving improvements in standards of living, income distribution, and reductions in poverty.

**Range of Arguments:**

- Greater market access and export-led growth  
  - Brazil’s and South Africa’s bilateral trade could expand from US 3.5 to US 5.2 billion by 2025. An increase in exports contributes to higher AD, possibly raising real GDP. More FDI inflows (US 46 billion in Brazil, US$4.8 billion in South Africa) can also foster technology transfers and job creation.  
  - However, structural constraints such as high youth unemployment in South Africa and political uncertainty in Brazil may limit the full benefits of export-led growth.  

- Improvements in resource allocation and productive efficiency  
  - Lower tariffs on goods such as automobiles, agriculture, and technology can allow each country to specialize in line with its comparative advantage. Brazil specializes in agricultural products like coffee and soybeans; South Africa focuses on minerals and manufactured goods. This specialization may enhance productive efficiency.  
  - Yet, negative externalities, particularly deforestation in Brazil and pollution in South Africa, can impose long-term social costs. The need for sustainable policies may divert financial resources away from immediate output gains, complicating development objectives.  

- Impact on unemployment and income distribution  
  - Trade expansion may create jobs in export sectors, helping reduce Brazil’s 11.3% unemployment and South Africa’s 32.6% unemployment rates. SMEs in both countries may benefit from preferential export credits, stimulating entrepreneurship and formal sector employment.  
  - In the short run, industries facing foreign competition might downsize, causing structural unemployment if workers cannot transition quickly. Income inequality may persist or worsen unless governments adopt effective retraining and welfare programs.  

- Financial sector integration and currency stability  
  - Cooperation in financial services and the formation of a Stabilization Fund can help hedge currency risks, encouraging investment. Stabilizing exchange rates could lower inflationary pressures from imported inputs.  
  - If the real or rand continues depreciating unpredictably, higher import prices can stoke inflation and undermine consumer purchasing power. Reduced real incomes would constrain longer-term economic development.  

**Evaluation and Conclusion:** 

 -  BSEPA can stimulate economic growth through increased trade volumes, FDI, and specialization by comparative advantage. This could lead to higher incomes, better technology transfer, and potential job creation.  
  - However, structural constraints like youth unemployment, political uncertainty, and negative externalities from mining/deforestation can hinder sustained development. Policy efforts, including retraining programs and environmental regulations, may be necessary to ensure that growth translates into broad-based improvements in welfare.  
  - If managed effectively, BSEPA may contribute positively to both economic growth and development in Brazil and South Africa; if not, gains in GDP might be offset by rising inequalities, environmental harm, or exchange rate uncertainties.

questions.specification:
Colombia is located in the northwestern region of South America. It is the world’s third-largest producer of coffee, accounting for around 10% of global supply. The Colombian National Coffee Growers Federation (FNC) estimates that approximately 500 000 families depend on coffee production for their livelihoods. In 2022, Colombia earned an estimated US $2.5 billion from coffee exports, representing about 8% of the country’s total export revenue.

Although the Colombian economy has shown signs of growth, income inequality remains a concern. With a Gini coefficient of about 0.50 in 2022, Colombia has one of the highest levels of income inequality in Latin America. The government is exploring new social welfare programs and potential reforms to its tax system to address these disparities.

Table 1 shows key macroeconomic indicators for Colombia between 2021 and 2022. It includes data on real GDP, population, income inequality, government spending, and tax revenue.

Table 1: Key Macroeconomic Indicators for Colombia, 2021–2022

| Indicator                                    | 2021                 | 2022                 |
|---------------------------------------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Real GDP (billion US$, constant 2015)       | 300                  | 330                  |
| Population (millions)                       | 49.5                 | 50.3                 |
| Gini coefficient                            | 0.51                 | 0.50                 |
| Official poverty rate (%)                   | 35                   | 34                   |
| Government spending (billion US$)           | 60                   | 63                   |
| Tax revenue (billion US$)                   | 52                   | 55                   |

Between January and July 2022, the price of Arabica coffee rose significantly, from US $1.20 per pound to US $1.50 per pound, partly due to strong global demand and weather-related supply constraints. Table 2 shows the changes in price, quantity demanded by the international market, and quantity supplied domestically in Colombia during this period (quantities are measured in millions of standard 60-kg bags of coffee).

Table 2: Price and Quantity Changes in the Colombian Coffee Market, Jan–Jul 2022

| Month    | Price (US$/lb) | Quantity Demanded (millions of 60-kg bags) | Quantity Supplied (millions of 60-kg bags) |
|----------|----------------|-------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| January  | 1.20           | 5.0                                       | 4.0                                       |
| July     | 1.50           | 4.7                                       | 4.3                                       |

In 2022, Colombia’s inflation rate was approximately 4.5%. The Ministry of Finance is considering increasing government investment in the coffee sector to boost production capacity and generate additional employment. Economists estimate a Keynesian multiplier of 1.4 for new investments in the coffee sector, meaning that each dollar invested could generate an additional US $0.40 in national income.

Meanwhile, there is also debate around Colombia’s tax system. The current corporate tax rate stands at 31%, and personal income tax rates are progressive, peaking at 39% for top earners. The standard VAT rate is 19% on most goods and services, although some necessities have lower or zero VAT.

parts.content: Calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for Colombian coffee between January and July 2022.
parts.markscheme:
• Percentage change in quantity demanded = [(4.7 − 5.0) ÷ 5.0] × 100 = ( − 0.3 / 5.0 ) × 100 = −6%  
• Percentage change in price = [(1.50 − 1.20) ÷ 1.20] × 100 = (0.30 / 1.20) × 100 = 25%  
• PED = ( −6% ) / 25% = −0.24 (absolute value 0.24)

parts.content: Explain why a rise in coffee prices may not lead to a significant reduction in global demand for Colombia’s coffee.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/f4e98160-6cb1-4a78-a14d-817a85112683)
• Coffee tends to have few close substitutes, making global demand relatively price inelastic.  
• Many consumers consider coffee a necessity, minimizing the impact of higher prices on demand.  
• Colombia’s reputation for high-quality coffee reduces consumers’ willingness to switch to alternatives. 
• Coffee expenditure is a relatively small portion of most consumers’ budgets, so a price change has a limited effect on demand.

parts.content: Using the data from Table 1, explain how a reduction in the Gini coefficient might indicate progress toward economic development in Colombia.
parts.markscheme:
• A decrease in the Gini coefficient from 0.51 to 0.50 signals a more even distribution of income.  
• This suggests that lower-income households may be receiving a larger share of total income.  
• Progress toward greater income equity can improve living standards and reduce poverty, important indicators of economic development.  
• The official poverty rate has fallen from 35% to 34%, consistent with the trend of lower inequality.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy that the Colombian government could implement to reduce income inequality while supporting the coffee sector.
parts.markscheme:
• Define income inequality as the unequal distribution of income among individuals or households, highlighted by Colombia’s Gini coefficient of 0.50.  
• Define a subsidy as financial assistance provided by the government to firms to lower their production costs and increase output.  
• Propose a targeted subsidy program for small- to medium-scale coffee producers that decreases production costs and encourages higher output to meet global demand, building on Colombia’s significant coffee export revenues of US \$2.5 billion.  
• Explain that subsidizing coffee farms can shift the supply curve to the right in the coffee market, leading to potentially lower prices, higher production, and increased incomes for coffee-growing families.  
• Describe how increased supply in a key export sector can generate export revenue, feed into higher GDP growth, and utilize the Keynesian multiplier of 1.4, so each US \$1 invested can add US \$0.40 in national income.  
• Highlight that increased incomes for coffee-related households could help reduce the official poverty rate (currently 34%) and narrow income inequality by delivering benefits directly to vulnerable groups dependent on coffee production.  
• Emphasize that a targeted government subsidy can work alongside a slightly more progressive direct tax structure (currently peaking at 39% for top earners) to redistribute gains from the coffee sector and fund social welfare programs.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/25b11567-83ee-4ad6-b083-3bbae1a2b751)
• Reference the Gini coefficient’s decline from 0.51 to 0.50, suggesting that a combination of direct support to farmers and progressive taxation can further reduce inequality.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/6353a38d-bdfe-4209-a3b8-f1e46440a709)
• Note that ensuring government spending remains sustainable at 63 billion US\$ compared with a 55 billion US\$ tax revenue requires careful budgeting to avoid excessive deficits.  
• Conclude that targeted subsidies for coffee producers, combined with complementary tax reforms, can foster inclusive economic growth, support a key export sector, and continue the downward trend in Colombia’s Gini coefficient.

questions.specification:
Brazil is the largest economy in Latin America, with a population of approximately 214 million people. Real GDP growth in 2022 was reported at 2.9%, partly driven by the agricultural sector and commodity exports. Income inequality remains high, reflected in a Gini coefficient of around 0.53. Despite its diversified economy, Brazil relies heavily on primary commodities such as coffee, soybeans, and iron ore for export revenue.

Coffee is a major export for Brazil and has historically been susceptible to fluctuations in global demand and changes in weather patterns. In 2022, unfavorable climate conditions in certain coffee-producing regions contributed to a lower-than-expected harvest, pushing up coffee prices internationally. Meanwhile, the Brazilian real (BRL) has undergone phases of depreciation against the US dollar over the past few years, affecting both export competitiveness and the cost of imported inputs.

Beyond agriculture, the Brazilian government faces the challenge of ensuring inclusive growth. Income inequality remains persistent, with approximately 21% of the population living below the national poverty line. While the government collects both direct and indirect taxes, efforts to reform the tax system continue, aimed at improving the efficiency of tax collection and reducing the burden on lower-income households.

Below are selected data tables with information on Brazil’s labor market, macroeconomic indicators, and coffee market conditions. You will use these data as well as the text above to answer the questions in Part (a) and Part (b).

**Table 1: Basic labor market data for Brazil (2022)**

| Population (millions) | Labor Force (millions) | Employed (millions) | Unemployed (millions) |
|-----------------------|------------------------|---------------------|-----------------------|
| 214                   | 108                    | 99                  | 9                     |

**Table 2: Selected macroeconomic indicators for Brazil**

| Year | Nominal GDP (billion US$) | GDP Deflator (Index, base year = 2021) |
|------|---------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| 2021 | 1620                      | 1.00                                   |
| 2022 | 1780                      | 1.05                                   |

**Table 3: Coffee market data for Brazil (2022)**

| Price of coffee (US$/lb) | Quantity demanded (thousand tonnes) |
|--------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| 2.80                     | 2250                                 |
| 3.20                     | 2100                                 |

Additional information:
- One tonne of coffee ≈ 2,204.6 lbs (pounds).  
- Approximately 70% of Brazil’s coffee production is exported.  
- The Gini coefficient for Brazil stands at 0.53.  
- The government collects both direct taxes (on personal and corporate income) and indirect taxes (such as ICMS on goods and services).

parts.content: Explain why an appreciation of the Brazilian real (BRL) might lead to a reduction in Brazil’s net exports of coffee.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/555ef7fe-8ed8-4e8f-a9a3-737b4995df2e)

- An appreciation increases the value of the BRL, making Brazilian coffee more expensive for international buyers.  
- Higher export prices reduce the quantity demanded of Brazilian coffee abroad, lowering export revenue.  
- At the same time, foreign goods become relatively cheaper in Brazil, increasing imports.  
- As a result, net exports (exports minus imports) of coffee decline because export revenue decreases while imports become more attractive.

Using the text above, explain how a rise in coffee exports might affect the current account of Brazil’s balance of payments.
parts.markscheme:
- Coffee exports represent a main source of export revenue, accounting for a significant portion of the goods section of the current account.  
- A rise in coffee exports increases export revenue, potentially helping to improve Brazil’s current account balance if imports remain unchanged.  
- Higher export earnings could offset the cost of imported inputs, especially given fluctuations in the Brazilian real.  
- This impact is particularly relevant since approximately 70% of Brazil’s coffee production is sold abroad, making the nation’s current account sensitive to changes in coffee export volumes.

parts.content: Using the data and your knowledge of economics, recommend one policy that the Brazilian government could implement to reduce income inequality. Justify your recommendation.
parts.markscheme:
- Define key terms related to income distribution, such as “income inequality” and “progressive taxation,” since Brazil has a Gini coefficient of 0.53, reflecting high inequality.  
- Propose the introduction of a more progressive tax system, where higher-income groups face increasing marginal tax rates, while lowering the reliance on indirect taxes that disproportionately affect lower-income households.  
- Clarify that reducing regressive taxation (like the ICMS on basic necessities) helps improve disposable incomes for the 21% of Brazilians living below the poverty line, lowering the Gini coefficient over time.  
- Highlight that progressive taxation can fund better-quality public services (health care, education, infrastructure) and directly address inequality, in line with Brazil’s objective of inclusive growth.  
- A more progressive tax system increases disposable income for low-income households, boosting consumption and causing aggregate demand to shift right (AD1 -> AD2). As a result, real output increases from Y to Yp and the price level rises from Pl1 to Pl2. The diagram shows how redistributive fiscal policy can reduce inequality while supporting short-run economic growth.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/20057930-7d3e-43d0-b1c6-b7758f5284cc)

- Note that although raising taxes on higher earners might potentially create disincentives to invest, the positive externalities from improved human capital and reduced poverty could outweigh these drawbacks.  
- Emphasize that, according to the text, large segments of the population remain at risk of poverty, so a policy focusing on equitable distribution and increased government revenue for social spending can lead to reduced inequality.  
- Argue that a progressive tax system requires careful implementation and enforcement to minimize tax evasion and maintain efficiency in tax collection, aligning with ongoing government efforts to reform Brazil’s tax structure.  
- Conclude that this policy could narrow income disparities, foster social stability, and potentially boost long-term economic growth by increasing the purchasing power of lower-income groups.

questions.specification:
## UZBEKISTAN’S ONGOING ECONOMIC TRANSFORMATION

Over the last decade, Uzbekistan, a doubly landlocked country in Central Asia with a population of approximately 34 million in 2021, has embarked on a series of ambitious reforms aimed at liberalizing its economy. Real GDP growth averaged around 5.8% per year between 2018 and 2021, supported by rising exports of cotton, gold, and natural gas. However, dependence on primary commodities poses risks to external balance, and the government has signaled intentions to diversify into textile manufacturing, tourism, and information technology.

State-led modernization has included partial price liberalization in agriculture, attracting foreign direct investment (FDI). A notable policy change involved easing bureaucratic barriers for foreign investors, enabling the launch of new small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs). While privatization of state assets in the textile and apparel industries has progressed, concerns remain regarding efficiency gains for local businesses. Anecdotal evidence shows that some domestically owned farms still struggle to access affordable credit, hampering investments in irrigation systems and modern equipment.

The government’s push toward a more open economy coincided with substantial exchange rate reforms. In 2017, the som was devalued, and the central bank began allowing more market-driven fluctuations. This policy aimed to improve exporters’ competitiveness and encourage remittance inflows. By 2021, the exchange rate stood at around UZS 10,600 per US dollar. Although this move pleased some international organizations, it also contributed to temporary spikes in imported inflation, which reached 12.4% in 2021.

Meanwhile, Uzbekistan contends with persistent poverty, especially in rural regions. While official estimates showed a poverty rate of nearly 15% in 2021, independent analyses suggest it could be higher when accounting for underemployment in the informal sector. The government has introduced targeted cash transfers and expanded vocational training to help break the so-called “poverty cycle,” in which low income, limited savings, and lack of education reinforce each other. Development agencies recommend further investments in human capital and infrastructure to tackle income inequality—whose Gini coefficient has hovered around 0.36 in recent years.

On the trade front, Uzbekistan has moved to reduce average tariff rates on specific imports, particularly machinery crucial for agribusiness modernization. In addition, the country is in deliberations to join several regional trade blocs to increase export opportunities. Critics argue that remaining administrative barriers continue to complicate cross-border exchanges and inflate the costs of imports, which could stifle productivity growth in domestic industries that rely on foreign technology.

Monetary policy remains aimed at managing high inflation. Although the central bank has softened some liquidity constraints to facilitate credit expansion, the policy rate has been kept relatively high to stem inflationary pressures. The government hopes that stable macroeconomic conditions, alongside structural reforms, will draw in greater inflows of both portfolio investments and FDI to diversify the economic base beyond raw materials.

Infrastructure improvements are also underway, including the construction of new road and railway links to neighboring countries. Advocates argue these projects enhance regional integration, while skeptics caution that excessive reliance on foreign loans could worsen external debt burdens. Moreover, concerns about corruption and inefficient public spending persist, calling for enhanced transparency measures.

Human development indicators reflect a mixed picture. Literacy rates exceed 99%, but the national unemployment rate for youth reached 9.1% in 2021. The government has responded by emphasizing job creation through SME support programs and rural development projects. Uzbekistan’s World Bank “Ease of Doing Business” ranking has improved significantly since 2016, yet some local entrepreneurs still cite unpredictable regulations and limited access to finance as ongoing constraints.

Although reforms are moving Uzbekistan toward a more market-oriented economy, policymakers face complex trade-offs among controlling inflation, safeguarding impoverished communities, and ensuring stable economic growth. The trajectory of these reforms—and especially their impact on poverty—will depend on consistent policy implementation, regional integration initiatives, and continued progress in governance and institutional capacity.

Below are two tables that provide more detailed information on Uzbekistan’s recent economic indicators and development progress:

**Table 1: Key Macroeconomic Indicators (2018–2021)**

| Indicator                       | 2018    | 2019    | 2020    | 2021    |
|---------------------------------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| Nominal GDP (US$ billion)       | 50.3    | 57.2    | 59.7    | 62.5    |

parts.content: (i) Define the term “bureaucratic barriers” (paragraph 2).
parts.markscheme:
Bureaucratic barriers  
A form of administrative or regulatory obstacles (such as licenses, permits, lengthy procedures) imposed by government agencies that increase costs and complexity, potentially slowing investment or business activity.

parts.content: (ii) Define the term “tariff” (paragraph 7).
parts.markscheme:
Tariff  
A tax imposed on imported goods and services, usually designed to protect domestic industries and/or raise government revenue by increasing import prices relative to domestic products.

parts.content: (i) Using information from Table 1, calculate the absolute increase in Uzbekistan’s nominal GDP (in US$ billions) from 2018 to 2021.
parts.markscheme:
Calculation of the absolute increase in nominal GDP  
• From Table 1, nominal GDP in 2018 was US$50.3 billion, and in 2021 it was US$62.5 billion.  
• Therefore, the absolute increase = 62.5 – 50.3 = US$12.2 billion.

parts.content: (ii) Sketch an AD/AS diagram to show how rising inflation (Table 1) could be influenced by changes in aggregate demand.
parts.markscheme:
AD/AS diagram to show rising inflation from increased aggregate demand  
• When aggregate demand (AD) increases (e.g., due to higher investment and exports), it shifts outward from AD1 to AD2.  
• An outward shift of AD in the short run, with aggregate supply (AS) relatively unchanged, leads to a higher general price level (inflation).  
• Graphically, the intersection of AD2 and AS is at a higher price level than the intersection of AD1 and AS.

parts.content: Using a demand-and-supply-of-currency diagram, explain how reducing bureaucratic barriers to FDI might affect the supply of foreign currency in Uzbekistan’s foreign exchange market.
parts.markscheme:
Reducing bureaucratic barriers to FDI and the supply of foreign currency in Uzbekistan’s foreign exchange market  
Reducing bureaucratic barriers makes it easier for foreign investors to set up operations in Uzbekistan. This likely increases FDI inflows, as more foreign currency enters the country (because foreign investors must convert foreign currency into som when investing). On a demand-and-supply-of-currency diagram, the supply curve of foreign currency shifts to the right, lowering the exchange rate (in terms of UZS per US$) or at least exerting downward pressure on it, assuming other factors remain constant.

parts.content: Using a monetary policy diagram (showing money supply and interest rates), explain how the central bank’s relatively high policy rate could affect inflation and economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
The central bank’s relatively high policy rate and its effects on inflation and growth  
Keeping a high policy rate discourages excessive borrowing and helps reduce inflationary pressure by dampening aggregate demand. However, it can also slow economic growth by discouraging investment and consumption financed by credit. In a monetary policy diagram (with interest rate on the vertical axis and quantity of money on the horizontal axis), a relatively high interest rate corresponds to a stricter monetary policy stance, controlling inflation but potentially reducing real GDP growth.

questions.specification:
**India's Strategic Pivot Toward Global Service Exports**

Over the last two decades, India has emerged as a major player in the global economy, primarily driven by its rapid expansion in the services sector and strategic trade liberalization. The Indian government’s focus on high-value software services, pharmaceuticals, and engineering goods has transformed the nation's economic landscape. By reducing tariffs on essential capital goods and fostering international trade agreements, India has successfully integrated itself into global value chains.

Economists argue that by specializing in sectors where it possesses a comparative advantage—such as information technology (IT) and business process outsourcing (BPO)—India has achieved significant efficiency gains and sustained economic growth. This specialization allows the economy to maximize the output from its large, English-speaking, and increasingly skilled workforce. However, a high dependency on a few key markets, particularly the United States and the European Union, poses structural risks if these trading partners experience economic downturns or shift toward protectionist policies.

To further boost its global competitiveness, the Indian government has sometimes utilized export subsidies and Special Economic Zones (SEZs). While these measures aim to support domestic firms and attract foreign investment, they can occasionally lead to trade disputes or retaliatory measures from other nations. Furthermore, while trade liberalization provides cheaper access to imported technology and machinery, domestic producers in less competitive sectors, like traditional manufacturing, often struggle to compete with low-cost imports.

A central goal of India's trade policy is to balance the immediate benefits of export-led growth with long-term macroeconomic stability. As the global economy faces inflationary pressures and fluctuating demand, policymakers must determine whether further deregulation or targeted protection of infant industries is the best path forward for sustainable development.

Table 1: India’s Major Export Categories (2022, in billion US$)

$$
\begin{array}{|l|r|}
\hline
\textbf{Export Category} & \textbf{Value (US\$ billion)} \\ \hline
\text{Software and IT Services} & 156.7 \\ \hline
\text{Engineering Goods} & 112.1 \\ \hline
\text{Pharmaceuticals} & 25.3 \\ \hline
\text{Gems and Jewelry} & 38.4 \\ \hline
\text{Other Exports} & 67.5 \\ \hline
\textbf{Total Exports} & \textbf{400.0} \\ \hline
\end{array}
$$

Table 2: India’s Primary Export Destinations (2022)

$$
\begin{array}{|l|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Country} & \textbf{Share of Total Exports (\%)} \\ \hline
\text{United States} & 18.0 \\ \hline
\text{United Arab Emirates} & 6.5 \\ \hline
\text{China} & 3.4 \\ \hline
\text{Bangladesh} & 3.0 \\ \hline
\text{Netherlands} & 2.5 \\ \hline
\text{Other Countries} & 66.6 \\ \hline
\end{array}
$$
